If no type constraint is set then a value of any type\nis accepted.\n\nWhile type constraints are optional, we recommend specifying them; they\ncan serve as helpful reminders for users of the module, and they\nallow Terraform to return a helpful error message if the wrong type is used.\n\nType constraints are created from a mixture of type keywords and type\nconstructors. The supported type keywords are:\n\n* `string`\n* `number`\n* `bool`\n\nThe type constructors allow you to specify complex types such as\ncollections:\n\n* `list()`\n* `set()`\n* `map()`\n* `object({ = , ... })`\n* `tuple([, ...])`\n\nThe keyword `any` may be used to indicate that any type is acceptable. For\nmore information on the meaning and behavior of these different types, as well\nas detailed information about automatic conversion of complex types, see\n[Type Constraints](/terraform/language/expressions/types).\n\nIf both the `type` and `default` arguments are specified, the given default\nvalue must be convertible to the specified type.\n\n### Input Variable Documentation\n\n[inpage-description]: #input-variable-documentation\n\nBecause the input variables of a module are part of its user interface, you can\nbriefly describe the purpose of each variable using the optional\n`description` argument:\n\n```hcl\nvariable \"image_id\" {\n type = string\n description = \"The id of the machine image (AMI) to use for the server.\"\n}\n```\n\nThe description should concisely explain the purpose\nof the variable and what kind of value is expected. This description string\nmight be included in documentation about the module, and so it should be written\nfrom the perspective of the user of the module rather than its maintainer. For\ncommentary for module maintainers, use comments.\n\n### Custom Validation Rules\n\n[inpage-validation]: #custom-validation-rules\n\n-> This feature was introduced in Terraform CLI v0.13.0.\n\nYou can specify custom validation rules for a particular variable by adding a `validation` block within the corresponding `variable` block. The example below checks whether the AMI ID has the correct syntax.\n\n```hcl\nvariable \"image_id\" {\n type = string\n description = \"The id of the machine image (AMI) to use for the server.\"\n\n validation {\n condition = length(var.image_id) > 4 && substr(var.image_id, 0, 4) == \"ami-\"\n error_message = \"The image_id value must be a valid AMI id, starting with \\\"ami-\\\".\"\n }\n}\n```\nRefer to [Custom Condition Checks](/terraform/language/expressions/custom-conditions#input-variable-validation) for more details.\n\n### Exclude values from state\n\n-> **Note**: Ephemeral variables are in public beta and available in Terraform v1.10 and later. The workflows of ephemeral variables are subject to change.\n\n[inpage-ephemeral]: #exclude-values-from-state\n\nSetting a variable as `ephemeral` makes it available during runtime, but Terraform omits ephemeral values from state and plan files. Marking an input variable as `ephemeral` is useful for data that only needs to exist temporarily, such as a short-lived token or session identifier.\n\nMark an input variable as ephemeral by setting the `ephemeral` argument to `true`:\n\n```hcl\nvariable \"session_token\" {\n type = string\n ephemeral = true\n}\n```\n\nEphemeral variables are available during the current Terraform operation, and Terraform does not store them in state or plan files. So, unlike [`sensitive`](#sensitive) inputs, Terraform ensures ephemeral values are not available beyond the lifetime of the current Terraform run.\n\nYou can only reference ephemeral variables in specific contexts or Terraform throws an error. The following are valid contexts for referencing ephemeral variables:\n* Another ephemeral variable\n* In [local values](/terraform/language/values/locals#ephemeral-values)\n* In [ephemeral resources](/terraform/language/resources/ephemeral)\n* In [ephemeral outputs](/terraform/language/values/outputs#ephemeral-avoid-storing-values-in-state-or-plan-files)\n\nIf another expression references an `ephemeral` variable, that expression implicitly becomes ephemeral.\n\n```hcl\nvariable \"password\" {\n type = string\n ephemeral = true\n}\n\nlocals {\n # local.database_password is implicitly ephemeral because \n # var.password is ephemeral.\n database_password = var.password\n}\n```\n\nThe `local.database_password` value is implicitly ephemeral because it depends on `var.password`.\n\n### Suppressing Values in CLI Output\n\n[inpage-sensitive]: #suppressing-values-in-cli-output\n\n> **Hands-on:** Try the [Protect Sensitive Input Variables](/terraform/tutorials/configuration-language/sensitive-variables?utm_source=WEBSITE&utm_medium=WEB_IO&utm_offer=ARTICLE_PAGE&utm_content=DOCS) tutorial.\n\nSetting a variable as `sensitive` prevents Terraform from showing its value in\nthe `plan` or `apply` output, when you use that variable elsewhere in your\nconfiguration.\n\nTerraform will still record sensitive values in the [state](/terraform/language/state),\nand so anyone who can access the state data will have access to the sensitive\nvalues in cleartext. If you want to omit a value from state, mark that value as [`ephemeral`](#ephemeral). For more information, refer to\n[Sensitive Data in State](/terraform/language/state/sensitive-data).\n\nDeclare a variable as sensitive by setting the `sensitive` argument to `true`:\n\n```hcl\nvariable \"user_information\" {\n type = object({\n name = string\n address = string\n })\n sensitive = true\n}\n\nresource \"some_resource\" \"a\" {\n name = var.user_information.name\n address = var.user_information.address\n}\n```\n\nAny expressions whose result depends on the sensitive variable will be treated\nas sensitive themselves, and so in the above example the two arguments of\n`resource \"some_resource\" \"a\"` will also be hidden in the plan output:\n\n```\nTerraform will perform the following actions:\n\n # some_resource.a will be created\n + resource \"some_resource\" \"a\" {\n + name = (sensitive value)\n + address = (sensitive value)\n }\n\nPlan: 1 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.\n```\n\nIn some cases where you use a sensitive variable inside a nested block, Terraform\nmay treat the entire block as redacted. This happens for resource types where\nall of the blocks of a particular type are required to be unique, and so\ndisclosing the content of one block might imply the content of a sibling block.\n\n```\n # some_resource.a will be updated in-place\n ~ resource \"some_resource\" \"a\" {\n ~ nested_block {\n # At least one attribute in this block is (or was) sensitive,\n # so its contents will not be displayed.\n }\n }\n```\n\nA provider can also\n[declare an attribute as sensitive](/terraform/plugin/sdkv2/best-practices/sensitive-state#using-the-sensitive-flag),\nwhich will cause Terraform to hide it from regular output regardless of how\nyou assign it a value. For more information, see\n[Sensitive Resource Attributes](/terraform/language/expressions/references#sensitive-resource-attributes).\n\nIf you use a sensitive value as part of an\n[output value](/terraform/language/values/outputs) then Terraform will require\nyou to also mark the output value itself as sensitive, to confirm that you\nintended to export it.\n\n#### Cases where Terraform may disclose a sensitive variable\n\nA `sensitive` variable is a configuration-centered concept, and values are sent to providers without any obfuscation. A provider error could disclose a value if that value is included in the error message. For example, a provider might return the following error even if \"foo\" is a sensitive value: `\"Invalid value 'foo' for field\"`\n\nIf a resource attribute is used as, or part of, the provider-defined resource id, an `apply` will disclose the value. In the example below, the `prefix` attribute has been set to a sensitive variable, but then that value (\"jae\") is later disclosed as part of the resource id:\n\n```\n # random_pet.animal will be created\n + resource \"random_pet\" \"animal\" {\n + id = (known after apply)\n + length = 2\n + prefix = (sensitive value)\n + separator = \"-\"\n }\n\nPlan: 1 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.\n\n...\n\nrandom_pet.animal: Creating...\nrandom_pet.animal: Creation complete after 0s [id=jae-known-mongoose]\n```\n\n### Disallowing Null Input Values\n\n[inpage-nullable]: #disallowing-null-input-values\n\n-> This feature is available in Terraform v1.1.0 and later.\n\nThe `nullable` argument in a variable block controls whether the module caller\nmay assign the value `null` to the variable.\n\n```hcl\nvariable \"example\" {\n type = string\n nullable = false\n}\n```\n\nThe default value for `nullable` is `true`. When `nullable` is `true`, `null`\nis a valid value for the variable, and the module configuration must always\naccount for the possibility of the variable value being `null`. Passing a\n`null` value as a module input argument will override any `default` value.\n\nSetting `nullable` to `false` ensures that the variable value will never be\n`null` within the module. If `nullable` is `false` and the variable has a\n`default` value, then Terraform uses the default when a module input argument is `null`.\n\nThe `nullable` argument only controls where the direct value of the variable may be `null`.\nFor variables of collection or structural types, such as lists or objects,\nthe caller may still use `null` in nested elements or attributes, as long as\nthe collection or structure itself is not null.\n\n## Using Input Variable Values\n\nWithin the module that declared a variable, its value can be accessed from\nwithin [expressions](/terraform/language/expressions) as `var.`,\nwhere `` matches the label given in the declaration block:\n\n-> **Note:** Input variables are _created_ by a `variable` block, but you\n_reference_ them as attributes on an object named `var`.\n\n```hcl\nresource \"aws_instance\" \"example\" {\n instance_type = \"t2.micro\"\n ami = var.image_id\n}\n```\n\nThe value assigned to a variable can only be accessed in expressions within\nthe module where it was declared.\n\n## Assigning Values to Root Module Variables\n\nWhen variables are declared in the root module of your configuration, they\ncan be set in a number of ways:\n\n* [In an HCP Terraform workspace](/terraform/cloud-docs/workspaces/variables).\n* Individually, with the `-var` command line option.\n* In variable definitions (`.tfvars`) files, either specified on the command line\n or automatically loaded.\n* As environment variables.\n\nThe following sections describe these options in more detail. This section does\nnot apply to _child_ modules, where values for input variables are instead\nassigned in the configuration of their parent module, as described in\n[_Modules_](/terraform/language/modules).\n\n### Variables on the Command Line\n\nTo specify individual variables on the command line, use the `-var` option\nwhen running the `terraform plan` and `terraform apply` commands:\n\n```\nterraform apply -var=\"image_id=ami-abc123\"\nterraform apply -var='image_id_list=[\"ami-abc123\",\"ami-def456\"]' -var=\"instance_type=t2.micro\"\nterraform apply -var='image_id_map={\"us-east-1\":\"ami-abc123\",\"us-east-2\":\"ami-def456\"}'\n```\n\nThe above examples show appropriate syntax for Unix-style shells, such as on\nLinux or macOS. For more information on shell quoting, including additional\nexamples for Windows Command Prompt, see\n[Input Variables on the Command Line](/terraform/cli/commands/plan#input-variables-on-the-command-line).\n\nYou can use the `-var` option multiple times in a single command to set several\ndifferent variables.\n\n\n\n### Variable Definitions (`.tfvars`) Files\n\nTo set lots of variables, it is more convenient to specify their values in\na _variable definitions file_ (with a filename ending in either `.tfvars`\nor `.tfvars.json`) and then specify that file on the command line with\n`-var-file`:\n\nLinux, Mac OS, and UNIX:\n\n```shell\nterraform apply -var-file=\"testing.tfvars\"\n```\n\nPowerShell:\n\n```shell\nterraform apply -var-file='testing.tfvars'\n```\n\nWindows `cmd.exe`:\n\n```shell\nterraform apply -var-file=\"testing.tfvars\"\n```\n\n-> **Note:** This is how HCP Terraform passes\n[workspace variables](/terraform/cloud-docs/workspaces/variables) to Terraform.\n\nA variable definitions file uses the same basic syntax as Terraform language\nfiles, but consists only of variable name assignments:\n\n```hcl\nimage_id = \"ami-abc123\"\navailability_zone_names = [\n \"us-east-1a\",\n \"us-west-1c\",\n]\n```\n\nTerraform also automatically loads a number of variable definitions files\nif they are present:\n\n* Files named exactly `terraform.tfvars` or `terraform.tfvars.json`.\n* Any files with names ending in `.auto.tfvars` or `.auto.tfvars.json`.\n\nFiles whose names end with `.json` are parsed instead as JSON objects, with\nthe root object properties corresponding to variable names:\n\n```json\n{\n \"image_id\": \"ami-abc123\",\n \"availability_zone_names\": [\"us-west-1a\", \"us-west-1c\"]\n}\n```\n\n### Environment Variables\n\nAs a fallback for the other ways of defining variables, Terraform searches\nthe environment of its own process for environment variables named `TF_VAR_`\nfollowed by the name of a declared variable.\n\nThis can be useful when running Terraform in automation, or when running a\nsequence of Terraform commands in succession with the same variables.\nFor example, at a `bash` prompt on a Unix system:\n\n```\n$ export TF_VAR_image_id=ami-abc123\n$ terraform plan\n...\n```\n\nOn operating systems where environment variable names are case-sensitive,\nTerraform matches the variable name exactly as given in configuration, and\nso the required environment variable name will usually have a mix of upper\nand lower case letters as in the above example.\n\n### Complex-typed Values\n\nWhen variable values are provided in a variable definitions file, you can use\nTerraform's usual syntax for\n[literal expressions](/terraform/language/expressions/types#literal-expressions)\nto assign complex-typed values, like lists and maps.\n\nSome special rules apply to the `-var` command line option and to environment\nvariables. For convenience, Terraform defaults to interpreting `-var` and\nenvironment variable values as literal strings, which need only shell quoting,\nand no special quoting for Terraform. For example, in a Unix-style shell:\n\n```\n$ export TF_VAR_image_id='ami-abc123'\n```\n\nHowever, if a root module variable uses a [type constraint](#type-constraints)\nto require a complex value (list, set, map, object, or tuple), Terraform will\ninstead attempt to parse its value using the same syntax used within variable\ndefinitions files, which requires careful attention to the string escaping rules\nin your shell:\n\n```\n$ export TF_VAR_availability_zone_names='[\"us-west-1b\",\"us-west-1d\"]'\n```\n\nFor readability, and to avoid the need to worry about shell escaping, we\nrecommend always setting complex variable values via variable definitions files.\nFor more information on quoting and escaping for `-var` arguments,\nsee\n[Input Variables on the Command Line](/terraform/cli/commands/plan#input-variables-on-the-command-line).\n\n### Values for Undeclared Variables\n\nIf you have defined a variable value, but not its corresponding `variable {}`\ndefinition, you may get an error or warning depending on how you have provided\nthat value.\n\nIf you provide values for undeclared variables defined as [environment variables](#environment-variables)\nyou will not get an error or warning. This is because environment variables may\nbe declared but not used in all configurations that might be run.\n\nIf you provide values for undeclared variables defined [in a file](#variable-definitions-tfvars-files)\nyou will get a warning. This is to help in cases where you have provided a variable\nvalue _meant_ for a variable declaration, but perhaps there is a mistake in the\nvalue definition. For example, the following configuration:\n\n```terraform\nvariable \"moose\" {\n type = string\n}\n```\n\nAnd the following `.tfvars` file:\n\n```hcl\nmosse = \"Moose\"\n```\n\nWill cause Terraform to warn you that there is no variable declared `\"mosse\"`, which can help\nyou spot this mistake.\n\nIf you use `.tfvars` files across multiple configurations and expect to continue to see this warning,\nyou can use the [`-compact-warnings`](/terraform/cli/commands/plan#compact-warnings)\noption to simplify your output.\n\nIf you provide values for undeclared variables on the [command line](#variables-on-the-command-line),\nTerraform will return an error. To avoid this error, either declare a variable block for the value, or remove\nthe variable value from your Terraform call.\n\n### Variable Definition Precedence\n\nThe above mechanisms for setting variables can be used together in any\ncombination. If the same variable is assigned multiple values, Terraform uses\nthe _last_ value it finds, overriding any previous values. Note that the same\nvariable cannot be assigned multiple values within a single source.\n\nTerraform loads variables in the following order, with later sources taking\nprecedence over earlier ones:\n\n* Environment variables\n* The `terraform.tfvars` file, if present.\n* The `terraform.tfvars.json` file, if present.\n* Any `*.auto.tfvars` or `*.auto.tfvars.json` files, processed in lexical order\n of their filenames.\n* Any `-var` and `-var-file` options on the command line, in the order they\n are provided. (This includes variables set by an HCP Terraform\n workspace.)\n\n~> **Important:** In Terraform 0.12 and later, variables with map and object\nvalues behave the same way as other variables: the last value found overrides\nthe previous values. This is a change from previous versions of Terraform, which\nwould _merge_ map values instead of overriding them.\n\n#### Variable precedence within Terraform tests\n\nWithin Terraform test files, you can specify variable values within\n`variables` blocks, either nested within `run` blocks or defined directly within\nthe file.\n\nVariables defined in this way take precedence over all other mechanisms during\ntest execution, with variables defined within `run` blocks taking precedence\nover those defined within the file.\n", "numLines": 583, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 583}}

1→---
     2→page_title: Ephemeral resource configuration reference
     3→description: Learn about ephemeral resource blocks that you can specify in Terraform configurations. Ephemeral resource blocks represent temporary resources that Terraform does not store in state.
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Ephemeral resource configuration reference
     7→
     8→This topic provides reference information for the `ephemeral` block. 
     9→
    10→-> **Note**: Ephemeral resources are in public beta and available in Terraform v1.10 and later. The workflows of ephemeral resources are subject to change.
    11→
    12→## Introduction
    13→
    14→Ephemeral resources are Terraform resources that are essentially temporary. Ephemeral resources have a unique lifecycle, and Terraform does not store them in its state. Each `ephemeral` block describes one or more ephemeral resources, such as a temporary password or connection to another system.
    15→
    16→<!-- After page exists, add: For information about how Terraform manages ephemeral resources, refer to
    17→[Epehemeral Values](/terraform/language/epehemeral-values). -->
    18→
    19→An `ephemeral` block declares an ephemeral resource of a specific type with a
    20→specific local name, much like a `resource` block. Terraform uses an ephemeral resource's name to
    21→refer to that resource in the same module, but an ephemeral resource's name has no meaning
    22→outside that module's scope.
    23→
    24→
    25→## Lifecycle
    26→
    27→The lifecycle of an ephemeral resource is different from resources and data sources. When Terraform provisions ephemeral resources, it performs the following steps:
    28→
    29→1. If Terraform needs to access the result of an ephemeral resource, it opens
    30→that ephemeral resource. For example, if Terraform opens an ephemeral resource for a Vault secret, the Vault provider obtains a lease and returns a secret. 
    31→
    32→1. If Terraform needs access to the ephemeral resource for longer than the
    33→remote system's enforced expiration time, Terraform asks the provider
    34→to renew it periodically. For example, if Terraform renews a Vault secret ephemeral resource, the Vault provider then calls Vault's lease renewal API endpoint to extend the expiration time.
    35→
    36→1. Once Terraform no longer needs an ephemeral resource, Terraform closes
    37→it. This happens after the providers that depend on a certain ephemeral resource
    38→complete all of their work for the current Terraform run phase. For example, closing a Vault secret ephemeral resource means the Vault provider explicitly ends the lease, allowing Vault to immediately revoke the associated credentials.
    39→
    40→Terraform follows these lifecycle steps for each instance of an ephemeral
    41→resource in a given configuration.
    42→
    43→## Dependency graph
    44→
    45→Ephemeral resources form nodes in Terraform's dependency graph, which interact similarly as resources and data sources. For example, when a resource or data source depends on an attribute of an ephemeral resource, Terraform automatically provisions the ephemeral resource first.
    46→
    47→## Configuration model
    48→
    49→Most of the arguments within the body of an `ephemeral` block are specific to the ephemeral resource you are defining. As with resources and data sources, each provider in the [Terraform Registry](https://registry.terraform.io/browse/providers) includes documentation for the ephemeral resources it supports, if any. An ephemeral resource type's documentation lists which arguments are available and how you should format your resource's values. 
    50→
    51→The following list outlines general field hierarchy, language-specific data types, and requirements in the `ephemeral` block. 
    52→
    53→- [`ephemeral`](#ephemeral): map
    54→  - [`attributes`](#ephemeral)
    55→  - [`meta-arguments`](#ephemeral)
    56→
    57→## Complete configuration
    58→
    59→An `ephemeral` block has the following form:
    60→
    61→```hcl
    62→ephemeral "<resource_type>" "<resource_name>" {
    63→  <attributes>
    64→  <meta-arguments>
    65→}
    66→```
    67→
    68→## Referencing ephemeral resources
    69→
    70→You can only reference ephemeral resources in specific ephemeral contexts or
    71→Terraform throws an error. The following are valid contexts for referencing
    72→ephemeral resources:
    73→
    74→* In another ephemeral resource
    75→* In [local values](/terraform/language/values/locals#ephemeral-values)
    76→* In [ephemeral variables](/terraform/language/values/variables#exclude-values-from-state)
    77→* In [ephemeral outputs](/terraform/language/values/outputs#ephemeral-avoid-storing-values-in-state-or-plan-files)
    78→* Configuring providers in the `provider` block
    79→* In [provisioner](/terraform/language/resources/provisioners/syntax) and [connection](/terraform/language/resources/provisioners/connection) blocks
    80→
    81→## Meta-arguments
    82→
    83→You can use the following meta-arguments with ephemeral resources to change the behavior of those resources. The following meta-arguments work the same way for resources, data sources, and ephemeral
    84→resources:
    85→
    86→- [`depends_on`, for specifying hidden dependencies](/terraform/language/meta-arguments/depends_on)
    87→- [`count`, for creating multiple resource instances according to a count](/terraform/language/meta-arguments/count)
    88→- [`for_each`, to create multiple instances according to a map or set of strings](/terraform/language/meta-arguments/for_each)
    89→- [`provider`, for selecting a non-default provider configuration](/terraform/language/meta-arguments/resource-provider)
    90→- [`lifecycle`, for lifecycle customizations](/terraform/language/meta-arguments/lifecycle)
    91→
    92→Ephemeral resources do not support the `provisioner` meta-argument.
    93→
    94→## Example
    95→
    96→The following example configures the `postgresql` provider with credentials from
    97→an ephemeral resource. Since these credentials are managed by an ephemeral resource, Terraform does not store them in your state or plan files.
    98→
    99→```hcl
   100→ephemeral "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "db_master" {
   101→  secret_id = data.aws_db_instance.example.master_user_secret[0].secret_arn
   102→}
   103→
   104→locals {
   105→  credentials = jsondecode(ephemeral.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.db_master.secret_string)
   106→}
   107→
   108→provider "postgresql" {
   109→  host     = data.aws_db_instance.example.address
   110→  port     = data.aws_db_instance.example.port
   111→  username = local.credentials["username"]
   112→  password = local.credentials["password"]
   113→}
   114→```
   115→

For example, when a resource or data source depends on an attribute of an ephemeral resource, Terraform automatically provisions the ephemeral resource first.\n\n## Configuration model\n\nMost of the arguments within the body of an `ephemeral` block are specific to the ephemeral resource you are defining. As with resources and data sources, each provider in the [Terraform Registry](https://registry.terraform.io/browse/providers) includes documentation for the ephemeral resources it supports, if any. An ephemeral resource type's documentation lists which arguments are available and how you should format your resource's values. \n\nThe following list outlines general field hierarchy, language-specific data types, and requirements in the `ephemeral` block. \n\n- [`ephemeral`](#ephemeral): map\n  - [`attributes`](#ephemeral)\n  - [`meta-arguments`](#ephemeral)\n\n## Complete configuration\n\nAn `ephemeral` block has the following form:\n\n```hcl\nephemeral \"<resource_type>\" \"<resource_name>\" {\n  <attributes>\n  <meta-arguments>\n}\n```\n\n## Referencing ephemeral resources\n\nYou can only reference ephemeral resources in specific ephemeral contexts or\nTerraform throws an error. The following are valid contexts for referencing\nephemeral resources:\n\n* In another ephemeral resource\n* In [local values](/terraform/language/values/locals#ephemeral-values)\n* In [ephemeral variables](/terraform/language/values/variables#exclude-values-from-state)\n* In [ephemeral outputs](/terraform/language/values/outputs#ephemeral-avoid-storing-values-in-state-or-plan-files)\n* Configuring providers in the `provider` block\n* In [provisioner](/terraform/language/resources/provisioners/syntax) and [connection](/terraform/language/resources/provisioners/connection) blocks\n\n## Meta-arguments\n\nYou can use the following meta-arguments with ephemeral resources to change the behavior of those resources. The following meta-arguments work the same way for resources, data sources, and ephemeral\nresources:\n\n- [`depends_on`, for specifying hidden dependencies](/terraform/language/meta-arguments/depends_on)\n- [`count`, for creating multiple resource instances according to a count](/terraform/language/meta-arguments/count)\n- [`for_each`, to create multiple instances according to a map or set of strings](/terraform/language/meta-arguments/for_each)\n- [`provider`, for selecting a non-default provider configuration](/terraform/language/meta-arguments/resource-provider)\n- [`lifecycle`, for lifecycle customizations](/terraform/language/meta-arguments/lifecycle)\n\nEphemeral resources do not support the `provisioner` meta-argument.\n\n## Example\n\nThe following example configures the `postgresql` provider with credentials from\nan ephemeral resource. Since these credentials are managed by an ephemeral resource, Terraform does not store them in your state or plan files.\n\n```hcl\nephemeral \"aws_secretsmanager_secret_version\" \"db_master\" {\n  secret_id = data.aws_db_instance.example.master_user_secret[0].secret_arn\n}\n\nlocals {\n  credentials = jsondecode(ephemeral.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.db_master.secret_string)\n}\n\nprovider \"postgresql\" {\n  host     = data.aws_db_instance.example.address\n  port     = data.aws_db_instance.example.port\n  username = local.credentials[\"username\"]\n  password = local.credentials[\"password\"]\n}\n```\n", "numLines": 115, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 115}}

Output values are similar to return values in programming languages. 11→ 12→> **Hands-on:** Try the [Output Data From 13→> Terraform](/terraform/tutorials/configuration-language/outputs) 14→> tutorial. 15→ 16→Output values have several uses: 17→ 18→- A child module can use outputs to expose a subset of its resource attributes 19→ to a parent module. 20→- A root module can use outputs to print certain values in the CLI output after 21→ running `terraform apply`. 22→- When using [remote state](/terraform/language/state/remote), root module outputs can be 23→ accessed by other configurations via a 24→ [`terraform_remote_state` data source](/terraform/language/state/remote-state-data). 25→ 26→Resource instances managed by Terraform each export attributes whose values 27→can be used elsewhere in configuration. Output values are a way to expose some 28→of that information to the user of your module. 29→ 30→-> **Note:** For brevity, output values are often referred to as just "outputs" 31→when the meaning is clear from context. 32→ 33→## Declaring an Output Value 34→ 35→Each output value exported by a module must be declared using an `output` 36→block: 37→ 38→```hcl 39→output "instance_ip_addr" { 40→ value = aws_instance.server.private_ip 41→} 42→``` 43→ 44→The label immediately after the `output` keyword is the name, which must be a 45→valid [identifier](/terraform/language/syntax/configuration#identifiers). In a root module, this name is 46→displayed to the user; in a child module, it can be used to access the output's 47→value. 48→ 49→The `value` argument takes an [expression](/terraform/language/expressions) 50→whose result is to be returned to the user. In this example, the expression 51→refers to the `private_ip` attribute exposed by an `aws_instance` resource 52→defined elsewhere in this module (not shown). Any valid expression is allowed 53→as an output value. 54→ 55→-> **Note:** Outputs are only rendered when Terraform applies your plan. Running 56→`terraform plan` will not render outputs. 57→ 58→## Accessing Child Module Outputs 59→ 60→In a parent module, outputs of child modules are available in expressions as 61→`module..`. For example, if a child module named 62→`web_server` declared an output named `instance_ip_addr`, you could access that 63→value as `module.web_server.instance_ip_addr`. 64→ 65→ 66→## Custom Condition Checks 67→ 68→You can use `precondition` blocks to specify guarantees about output data. The following examples creates a precondition that checks whether the EC2 instance has an encrypted root volume. 69→ 70→```hcl 71→output "api_base_url" { 72→ value = "https://${aws_instance.example.private_dns}:8433/" 73→ 74→ # The EC2 instance must have an encrypted root volume. 75→ precondition { 76→ condition = data.aws_ebs_volume.example.encrypted 77→ error_message = "The server's root volume is not encrypted." 78→ } 79→} 80→``` 81→ 82→Custom conditions can help capture assumptions, helping future maintainers understand the configuration design and intent. They also return useful information about errors earlier and in context, helping consumers more easily diagnose issues in their configurations. 83→ 84→Refer to [Custom Condition Checks](/terraform/language/expressions/custom-conditions#preconditions-and-postconditions) for more details. 85→ 86→## Optional Arguments 87→ 88→`output` blocks can optionally include `description`, `sensitive`, `ephemeral`, and `depends_on` arguments, which are described in the following sections. 89→ 90→ 91→ 92→### `description` — Output Value Documentation 93→ 94→Because the output values of a module are part of its user interface, you can 95→briefly describe the purpose of each value using the optional `description` 96→argument: 97→ 98→```hcl 99→output "instance_ip_addr" { 100→ value = aws_instance.server.private_ip 101→ description = "The private IP address of the main server instance." 102→} 103→``` 104→ 105→The description should concisely explain the 106→purpose of the output and what kind of value is expected. This description 107→string might be included in documentation about the module, and so it should be 108→written from the perspective of the user of the module rather than its 109→maintainer. For commentary for module maintainers, use comments. 110→ 111→ 112→ 113→### `ephemeral` — Avoid storing values in state or plan files 114→ 115→-> **Note**: Ephemeral outputs are in public beta and available in Terraform v1.10 and later. The workflows of ephemeral outputs are subject to change. 116→ 117→You can mark `output` values as `ephemeral` in child modules to pass ephemeral values between modules while avoiding persisting those values to state or plan files. This is useful for managing credentials, tokens, or other temporary resources you do not want to store in Terraform state files. 118→ 119→You can mark an `output` in a child module as ephemeral by setting the `ephemeral` attribute to `true`: 120→ 121→```hcl 122→# modules/db/main.tf 123→ 124→output "secret_id" { 125→ value = aws_secretsmanager_secret.secret_id 126→ description = "Temporary secret ID for accessing database in AWS." 127→ ephemeral = true 128→} 129→``` 130→ 131→Terraform has access to the value of `output` blocks during plan and apply operations. At the end of a plan or apply operation, Terraform does not persist the value of any ephemeral outputs. 132→ 133→You can only reference ephemeral outputs in specific contexts or Terraform throws an error. The following are valid contexts for referencing ephemeral outputs: 134→* In another child module's ephemeral `output` block 135→* In [ephemeral variables](/terraform/language/values/variables#exclude-values-from-state) 136→* In [ephemeral resources](/terraform/language/resources/ephemeral) 137→ 138→Note that you cannot set an `output` value as `ephemeral` in the root module. 139→ 140→ 141→ 142→ 143→### `sensitive` — Suppressing Values in CLI Output 144→ 145→An output can be marked as containing sensitive material using the optional 146→`sensitive` argument: 147→ 148→```hcl 149→output "db_password" { 150→ value = aws_db_instance.db.password 151→ description = "The password for logging in to the database." 152→ sensitive = true 153→} 154→``` 155→ 156→Terraform will hide values marked as sensitive in the messages from 157→`terraform plan` and `terraform apply`. In the following scenario, our root 158→module has an output declared as sensitive and a module call with a 159→sensitive output, which we then use in a resource attribute. 160→ 161→```hcl 162→# main.tf 163→ 164→module "foo" { 165→ source = "./mod" 166→} 167→ 168→resource "test_instance" "x" { 169→ some_attribute = module.foo.a # resource attribute references a sensitive output 170→} 171→ 172→output "out" { 173→ value = "xyz" 174→ sensitive = true 175→} 176→ 177→# mod/main.tf, our module containing a sensitive output 178→ 179→output "a" { 180→ value = "secret" 181→ sensitive = true 182→} 183→``` 184→ 185→When we run a plan or apply, the sensitive value is redacted from output: 186→ 187→``` 188→Terraform will perform the following actions: 189→ 190→ # test_instance.x will be created 191→ + resource "test_instance" "x" { 192→ + some_attribute = (sensitive value) 193→ } 194→ 195→Plan: 1 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy. 196→ 197→Changes to Outputs: 198→ + out = (sensitive value) 199→``` 200→ 201→Terraform will still record sensitive values in the [state](/terraform/language/state), 202→and so anyone who can access the state data will have access to the sensitive 203→values in cleartext. For more information, see 204→[Sensitive Data in State](/terraform/language/state/sensitive-data). 205→ 206→ 207→ 208→### `depends_on` — Explicit Output Dependencies 209→ 210→Since output values are just a means for passing data out of a module, it is 211→usually not necessary to worry about their relationships with other nodes in 212→the dependency graph. 213→ 214→However, when a parent module accesses an output value exported by one of its 215→child modules, the dependencies of that output value allow Terraform to 216→correctly determine the dependencies between resources defined in different 217→modules. 218→ 219→Just as with 220→[resource dependencies](/terraform/language/resources/behavior#resource-dependencies), 221→Terraform analyzes the `value` expression for an output value and automatically 222→determines a set of dependencies, but in less-common cases there are 223→dependencies that cannot be recognized implicitly. When using it, 240→always include a comment explaining why it is being used, to help future 241→maintainers understand the purpose of the additional dependency. 242→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. Output values are similar to return values in programming languages.\n\n> **Hands-on:** Try the [Output Data From\n> Terraform](/terraform/tutorials/configuration-language/outputs)\n> tutorial.\n\nOutput values have several uses:\n\n- A child module can use outputs to expose a subset of its resource attributes\n to a parent module.\n- A root module can use outputs to print certain values in the CLI output after\n running `terraform apply`.\n- When using [remote state](/terraform/language/state/remote), root module outputs can be\n accessed by other configurations via a\n [`terraform_remote_state` data source](/terraform/language/state/remote-state-data).\n\nResource instances managed by Terraform each export attributes whose values\ncan be used elsewhere in configuration. Output values are a way to expose some\nof that information to the user of your module.\n\n-> **Note:** For brevity, output values are often referred to as just \"outputs\"\nwhen the meaning is clear from context.\n\n## Declaring an Output Value\n\nEach output value exported by a module must be declared using an `output`\nblock:\n\n```hcl\noutput \"instance_ip_addr\" {\n value = aws_instance.server.private_ip\n}\n```\n\nThe label immediately after the `output` keyword is the name, which must be a\nvalid [identifier](/terraform/language/syntax/configuration#identifiers). In a root module, this name is\ndisplayed to the user; in a child module, it can be used to access the output's\nvalue.\n\nThe `value` argument takes an [expression](/terraform/language/expressions)\nwhose result is to be returned to the user. In this example, the expression\nrefers to the `private_ip` attribute exposed by an `aws_instance` resource\ndefined elsewhere in this module (not shown). Any valid expression is allowed\nas an output value.\n\n-> **Note:** Outputs are only rendered when Terraform applies your plan. Running\n`terraform plan` will not render outputs.\n\n## Accessing Child Module Outputs\n\nIn a parent module, outputs of child modules are available in expressions as\n`module..`. For example, if a child module named\n`web_server` declared an output named `instance_ip_addr`, you could access that\nvalue as `module.web_server.instance_ip_addr`.\n\n\n## Custom Condition Checks\n\nYou can use `precondition` blocks to specify guarantees about output data. The following examples creates a precondition that checks whether the EC2 instance has an encrypted root volume.\n\n```hcl\noutput \"api_base_url\" {\n value = \"https://${aws_instance.example.private_dns}:8433/\"\n\n # The EC2 instance must have an encrypted root volume.\n precondition {\n condition = data.aws_ebs_volume.example.encrypted\n error_message = \"The server's root volume is not encrypted.\"\n }\n}\n```\n\nCustom conditions can help capture assumptions, helping future maintainers understand the configuration design and intent. They also return useful information about errors earlier and in context, helping consumers more easily diagnose issues in their configurations.\n\nRefer to [Custom Condition Checks](/terraform/language/expressions/custom-conditions#preconditions-and-postconditions) for more details.\n\n## Optional Arguments\n\n`output` blocks can optionally include `description`, `sensitive`, `ephemeral`, and `depends_on` arguments, which are described in the following sections.\n\n\n\n### `description` — Output Value Documentation\n\nBecause the output values of a module are part of its user interface, you can\nbriefly describe the purpose of each value using the optional `description`\nargument:\n\n```hcl\noutput \"instance_ip_addr\" {\n value = aws_instance.server.private_ip\n description = \"The private IP address of the main server instance.\"\n}\n```\n\nThe description should concisely explain the\npurpose of the output and what kind of value is expected. This description\nstring might be included in documentation about the module, and so it should be\nwritten from the perspective of the user of the module rather than its\nmaintainer. For commentary for module maintainers, use comments.\n\n\n\n### `ephemeral` — Avoid storing values in state or plan files\n\n-> **Note**: Ephemeral outputs are in public beta and available in Terraform v1.10 and later. The workflows of ephemeral outputs are subject to change.\n\nYou can mark `output` values as `ephemeral` in child modules to pass ephemeral values between modules while avoiding persisting those values to state or plan files. This is useful for managing credentials, tokens, or other temporary resources you do not want to store in Terraform state files.\n\nYou can mark an `output` in a child module as ephemeral by setting the `ephemeral` attribute to `true`:\n\n```hcl\n# modules/db/main.tf\n\noutput \"secret_id\" {\n value = aws_secretsmanager_secret.secret_id\n description = \"Temporary secret ID for accessing database in AWS.\"\n ephemeral = true\n}\n```\n\nTerraform has access to the value of `output` blocks during plan and apply operations. At the end of a plan or apply operation, Terraform does not persist the value of any ephemeral outputs.\n\nYou can only reference ephemeral outputs in specific contexts or Terraform throws an error. The following are valid contexts for referencing ephemeral outputs:\n* In another child module's ephemeral `output` block\n* In [ephemeral variables](/terraform/language/values/variables#exclude-values-from-state)\n* In [ephemeral resources](/terraform/language/resources/ephemeral)\n\nNote that you cannot set an `output` value as `ephemeral` in the root module.\n\n\n\n\n### `sensitive` — Suppressing Values in CLI Output\n\nAn output can be marked as containing sensitive material using the optional\n`sensitive` argument:\n\n```hcl\noutput \"db_password\" {\n value = aws_db_instance.db.password\n description = \"The password for logging in to the database.\"\n sensitive = true\n}\n```\n\nTerraform will hide values marked as sensitive in the messages from\n`terraform plan` and `terraform apply`. In the following scenario, our root\nmodule has an output declared as sensitive and a module call with a\nsensitive output, which we then use in a resource attribute.\n\n```hcl\n# main.tf\n\nmodule \"foo\" {\n source = \"./mod\"\n}\n\nresource \"test_instance\" \"x\" {\n some_attribute = module.foo.a # resource attribute references a sensitive output\n}\n\noutput \"out\" {\n value = \"xyz\"\n sensitive = true\n}\n\n# mod/main.tf, our module containing a sensitive output\n\noutput \"a\" {\n value = \"secret\"\n sensitive = true\n}\n```\n\nWhen we run a plan or apply, the sensitive value is redacted from output:\n\n```\nTerraform will perform the following actions:\n\n # test_instance.x will be created\n + resource \"test_instance\" \"x\" {\n + some_attribute = (sensitive value)\n }\n\nPlan: 1 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.\n\nChanges to Outputs:\n + out = (sensitive value)\n```\n\nTerraform will still record sensitive values in the [state](/terraform/language/state),\nand so anyone who can access the state data will have access to the sensitive\nvalues in cleartext. For more information, see\n[Sensitive Data in State](/terraform/language/state/sensitive-data).\n\n\n\n### `depends_on` — Explicit Output Dependencies\n\nSince output values are just a means for passing data out of a module, it is\nusually not necessary to worry about their relationships with other nodes in\nthe dependency graph.\n\nHowever, when a parent module accesses an output value exported by one of its\nchild modules, the dependencies of that output value allow Terraform to\ncorrectly determine the dependencies between resources defined in different\nmodules.\n\nJust as with\n[resource dependencies](/terraform/language/resources/behavior#resource-dependencies),\nTerraform analyzes the `value` expression for an output value and automatically\ndetermines a set of dependencies, but in less-common cases there are\ndependencies that cannot be recognized implicitly. In these rare cases, the\n`depends_on` argument can be used to create additional explicit dependencies:\n\n```hcl\noutput \"instance_ip_addr\" {\n value = aws_instance.server.private_ip\n description = \"The private IP address of the main server instance.\"\n\n depends_on = [\n # Security group rule must be created before this IP address could\n # actually be used, otherwise the services will be unreachable.\n aws_security_group_rule.local_access,\n ]\n}\n```\n\nThe `depends_on` argument should be used only as a last resort. When using it,\nalways include a comment explaining why it is being used, to help future\nmaintainers understand the purpose of the additional dependency.\n", "numLines": 242, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 242}}

---
> When using [remote state](/terraform/language/state/remote), state is only ever held in memory when used by Terraform. It may be encrypted at rest, but this depends on the specific remote state backend.
> 
> ## Ephemeral data
> 
> -> **Note**: Ephemeral variables, outputs, and resources are in public beta and available in Terraform v1.10 and later. The workflows of ephemeral values are subject to change.
> 
> Terraform allows you to mark variables and outputs as ephemeral. Providers can also support specific `ephemeral` resources. Ephemerality in Terraform means that the data of a block is available during runtime, but Terraform does not write that data to state or plan files. The `ephemeral` property is helpful when managing credentials, tokens, or other temporary resources you do not want to store in Terraform state files.
> 
> Learn more about defining ephemeral [input variables](/terraform/language/values/variables#exclude-values-from-state), [outputs](/terraform/language/values/outputs#ephemeral-avoid-storing-values-in-state-or-plan-files), and [resources](/terraform/language/resources/ephemeral).
21,22c26
< If you manage any sensitive data with Terraform (like database passwords, user
< passwords, or private keys), treat the state itself as sensitive data.
---
> Treat the state as sensitive data if you manage secret credentials like database passwords, user passwords, or private keys with Terraform. You can also mark your sensitive data in variables as `ephemeral` to prevent Terraform from writing those variables to your state and plan files.

1→---
     2→page_title: ephemeralasnull function reference - Functions - Configuration Language  
     3→description: |-  
     4→  The `ephemeralasnull` function accepts an ephemeral value and returns null.  
     5→---
     6→
     7→# `ephemeralasnull` function reference
     8→
     9→-> **Note**: The `ephemeralasnull` function is in public beta and available in Terraform v1.10 and later. The workflows of `ephemeralasnull` are subject to change.
    10→
    11→
    12→This topic provides reference information about the `ephemeralasnull` function. The `ephemeralasnull` function accepts an ephemeral value and returns `null`. 
    13→
    14→## Introduction
    15→
    16→You can use the `ephemeralasnull` function to nullify ephemeral values. For example, if you pass an object with a nested ephemeral value to `ephemeralasnull`,it nullifies any ephemeral values within that object.
    17→
    18→## Syntax
    19→
    20→Use the `ephemeralasnull` function with the following syntax:
    21→
    22→```hcl
    23→ephemeralasnull(var.my_ephemeral_value)
    24→```
    25→
    26→In the following example, you can pass the `ephemeralasnull` function an ephemeral value, and the function returns `null`:
    27→
    28→```hcl
    29→variable "example" {
    30→  type      = string
    31→  default   = "test"
    32→  ephemeral = true
    33→}
    34→
    35→# This output returns null.
    36→output "example_output" {
    37→  value       = ephemeralasnull(var.example)
    38→}
    39→```
    40→
    41→For more information on which Terraform blocks can be ephemeral, refer to ephemeral [inputs](/terraform/language/values/variables#exclude-values-from-state), [outputs](/terraform/language/values/outputs#ephemeral-avoid-storing-values-in-state-or-plan-files), and [resources](/terraform/language/resources/ephemeral).
    42→
    43→## Example use case
    44→
    45→The following example shows how you can use the  `ephemeralasnull` function to intake a map that contains some ephemeral values, and output the values that are not ephemeral.
    46→
    47→```hcl
    48→variable "session_token" {
    49→  type      = string
    50→  default   = "test"
    51→  ephemeral = true
    52→}
    53→
    54→variable "configuration" {
    55→  type = map(string)
    56→  default = {
    57→    "env" = "development"
    58→  }
    59→}
    60→
    61→# This is a contrived example, but this pattern works with any object that is a mix of ephemeral and non-ephemeral values. 
    62→locals {
    63→  configuration_with_token = merge(
    64→    var.configuration,
    65→    { "session_token" = var.session_token }
    66→  )
    67→  ephemeral = true
    68→}
    69→
    70→output "configuration_settings" {
    71→# Using ephemeralasnull enables you to output the non-ephemeral values.
    72→  value       = ephemeralasnull(local.things_with_token)
    73→  description = "Environment setting."
    74→}
    75→```
    76→
    77→When you apply the above configuration, Terraform returns the `ephemeral` values in `locals.configuration_settings` as `null`. This lets you output the non-ephemeral value `env`.
    78→
    79→```hcl
    80→Outputs:
    81→
    82→configuration_settings = {
    83→  "env" = "development"
    84→  "session_token" = tostring(null)
    85→}
    86→```

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

The workflows of `ephemeralasnull` are subject to change.\n\n\nThis topic provides reference information about the `ephemeralasnull` function. The `ephemeralasnull` function accepts an ephemeral value and returns `null`. \n\n## Introduction\n\nYou can use the `ephemeralasnull` function to nullify ephemeral values. For example, if you pass an object with a nested ephemeral value to `ephemeralasnull`,it nullifies any ephemeral values within that object.\n\n## Syntax\n\nUse the `ephemeralasnull` function with the following syntax:\n\n```hcl\nephemeralasnull(var.my_ephemeral_value)\n```\n\nIn the following example, you can pass the `ephemeralasnull` function an ephemeral value, and the function returns `null`:\n\n```hcl\nvariable \"example\" {\n  type      = string\n  default   = \"test\"\n  ephemeral = true\n}\n\n# This output returns null.\noutput \"example_output\" {\n  value       = ephemeralasnull(var.example)\n}\n```\n\nFor more information on which Terraform blocks can be ephemeral, refer to ephemeral [inputs](/terraform/language/values/variables#exclude-values-from-state), [outputs](/terraform/language/values/outputs#ephemeral-avoid-storing-values-in-state-or-plan-files), and [resources](/terraform/language/resources/ephemeral).\n\n## Example use case\n\nThe following example shows how you can use the  `ephemeralasnull` function to intake a map that contains some ephemeral values, and output the values that are not ephemeral.\n\n```hcl\nvariable \"session_token\" {\n  type      = string\n  default   = \"test\"\n  ephemeral = true\n}\n\nvariable \"configuration\" {\n  type = map(string)\n  default = {\n    \"env\" = \"development\"\n  }\n}\n\n# This is a contrived example, but this pattern works with any object that is a mix of ephemeral and non-ephemeral values. \nlocals {\n  configuration_with_token = merge(\n    var.configuration,\n    { \"session_token\" = var.session_token }\n  )\n  ephemeral = true\n}\n\noutput \"configuration_settings\" {\n# Using ephemeralasnull enables you to output the non-ephemeral values.\n  value       = ephemeralasnull(local.things_with_token)\n  description = \"Environment setting.\"\n}\n```\n\nWhen you apply the above configuration, Terraform returns the `ephemeral` values in `locals.configuration_settings` as `null`. This lets you output the non-ephemeral value `env`.\n\n```hcl\nOutputs:\n\nconfiguration_settings = {\n  \"env\" = \"development\"\n  \"session_token\" = tostring(null)\n}\n```", "numLines": 86, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 86}}

arguments.command:
for f in $(find /workspace/terraform-v1.9.0/website/ -size +0c -name "*.mdx" 2>/dev/null); do
  rel="${f#/workspace/terraform-v1.9.0/}"
  v10="/workspace/terraform-v1.10.0/$rel"
  if [ -f "$v10" ]; then
    result=$(diff "$f" "$v10" 2>/dev/null)
    if [ -n "$result" ]; then
      echo "=== DIFF: $rel ==="
      echo "$result" | head -30
      echo ""
    fi
  fi
done 2>/dev/null | head -200

> ## Ephemeral values
> 
> -> **Note**: Ephemeral local values are in public beta and available in Terraform v1.10 and later. The workflows of ephemeral locals are subject to change.
> 
> Local values implicitly become ephemeral if you reference an ephemeral value when you assign that local a value. For example, you can create a local that references an ephemeral `service_token`.
> 
> ```hcl
> variable "service_name" {
>   type    = string
>   default = "forum"
> }
> 
> variable "environment" {
>   type    = string
>   default = "dev"
> }
> 
> variable "service_token" {
>   type      = string
>   ephemeral = true
> }
> 
> locals {
>   service_tag   = "${var.service_name}-${var.environment}"
>   session_token = "${var.service_name}:${var.service_token}"
> }
> ```
> 
> The `local.session_token` value is implicitly ephemeral because it relies on an ephemeral variable.
>

> * [`ephemeral`][inpage-ephemeral] - This variable is available during runtime, but not written to state or plan files.
177a179,220
> ### Exclude values from state
> 
> -> **Note**: Ephemeral variables are in public beta and available in Terraform v1.10 and later. The workflows of ephemeral variables are subject to change.
> 
> [inpage-ephemeral]: #exclude-values-from-state
> 
> Setting a variable as `ephemeral` makes it available during runtime, but Terraform omits ephemeral values from state and plan files. Marking an input variable as `ephemeral` is useful for data that only needs to exist temporarily, such as a short-lived token or session identifier.
> 
> Mark an input variable as ephemeral by setting the `ephemeral` argument to `true`:
> 
> ```hcl
> variable "session_token" {
>   type      = string
>   ephemeral = true
> }
> ```
> 
> Ephemeral variables are available during the current Terraform operation, and Terraform does not store them in state or plan files. So, unlike [`sensitive`](#sensitive) inputs, Terraform ensures ephemeral values are not available beyond the lifetime of the current Terraform run.
> 
> You can only reference ephemeral variables in specific contexts or Terraform throws an error. The following are valid contexts for referencing ephemeral variables:
> * Another ephemeral variable
> * In [local values](/terraform/language/values/locals#ephemeral-values)
> * In [ephemeral resources](/terraform/language/resources/ephemeral)
> * In [ephemeral outputs](/terraform/language/values/outputs#ephemeral-avoid-storing-values-in-state-or-plan-files)
> 
> If another expression references an `ephemeral` variable, that expression implicitly becomes ephemeral.
> 
> ```hcl
> variable "password" {
>   type      = string
>   ephemeral = true
> }
> 
> locals {
>   # local.database_password is implicitly ephemeral because 
>   # var.password is ephemeral.
>   database_password = var.password
> }
> ```
> 
> The `local.database_password` value is implicitly ephemeral because it depends on `var.password`.
>

297: var assumeRoleSchema = singleNestedAttribute{
298: 	Attributes: map[string]schemaAttribute{
299: 		"role_arn": stringAttribute{
300: 			configschema.Attribute{
301: 				Type:        cty.String,
302: 				Required:    true,
303: 				Description: "The role to be assumed.",
304: 			},
305: 			validateString{
306: 				Validators: []stringValidator{
307: 					validateStringNotEmpty,
308: 					validateARN(
309: 						validateIAMRoleARN,
310: 					),
311: 				},
312: 			},
313: 		},
314: 
315: 		"duration": stringAttribute{
316: 			configschema.Attribute{
317: 				Type:        cty.String,
318: 				Optional:    true,
319: 				Description: "The duration, between 15 minutes and 12 hours, of the role session. Valid time units are ns, us (or µs), ms, s, h, or m.",
320: 			},
321: 			validateString{
322: 				Validators: []stringValidator{
323: 					validateDuration(
324: 						validateDurationBetween(15*time.Minute, 12*time.Hour),
325: 					),
326: 				},
327: 			},
328: 		},
329: 
330: 		"external_id": stringAttribute{
331: 			configschema.Attribute{
332: 				Type:        cty.String,
333: 				Optional:    true,
334: 				Description: "The external ID to use when assuming the role",
335: 			},
336: 			validateString{
337: 				Validators: []stringValidator{
338: 					validateStringLenBetween(2, 1224),
339: 					validateStringMatches(
340: 						regexp.MustCompile(`^[\w+=,.@:\/\-]*$`),
341: 						`Value can only contain letters, numbers, or the following characters: =,.@/-`,
342: 					),
343: 				},
344: 			},
345: 		},
346: 
347: 		"policy": stringAttribute{
348: 			configschema.Attribute{

{"content":"1: // Copyright (c) HashiCorp, Inc.
2: // SPDX-License-Identifier: BUSL-1.1
3: 
4: package marks
5: 
6: import (
7: 	"github.com/zclconf/go-cty/cty"
8: )
9: 
10: // valueMarks allow creating strictly typed values for use as cty.Value marks.
11: // Each distinct mark value must be a constant in this package whose value
12: // is a valueMark whose underlying string matches the name of the variable.
13: type valueMark string
14: 
15: func (m valueMark) GoString() string {
16: 	return "marks." + string(m)
17: }
18: 
19: // Has returns true if and only if the cty.Value has the given mark.
20: func Has(val cty.Value, mark valueMark) bool {
21: 	return val.HasMark(mark)
22: }
23: 
24: // Contains returns true if the cty.Value or any any value within it contains
25: // the given mark.
26: func Contains(val cty.Value, mark valueMark) bool {
27: 	ret := false
28: 	cty.Walk(val, func(_ cty.Path, v cty.Value) (bool, error) {
29: 		if v.HasMark(mark) {
30: 			ret = true
31: 			return false, nil
32: 		}
33: 		return true, nil
34: 	})
35: 	return ret
36: }
37: 
38: // Sensitive indicates that this value is marked as sensitive in the context of
39: // Terraform.
40: const Sensitive = valueMark("Sensitive")
41: 
42: // Ephemeral indicates that a value exists only in memory during a single
43: // phase, and thus cannot persist between phases or between rounds.
44: //
45: // Ephemeral values can be used only in locations that don't require Terraform
46: // to persist them as part of artifacts such as state snapshots or saved plan
47: // files.
48: const Ephemeral = valueMark("Ephemeral")
49: 
50: // TypeType is used to indicate that the value contains a representation of
51: // another value's type. This is part of the implementation of the console-only
52: // `type` function.
53: const TypeType = valueMark("TypeType")
54: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-benchmarks/terraform--cd1e42f7@b73b7ba57020d9b12addb8ee0ea404d69c5d9dc8/-/blob/internal/lang/marks/marks.go"}
{"blocks":[]}

1→---
     2→page_title: Conditional Expressions - Configuration Language
     3→description: >-
     4→  Conditional expressions select one of two values. You can use them to define
     5→  defaults to replace invalid values.
     6→---
     7→
     8→# Conditional Expressions
     9→
    10→A _conditional expression_ uses the value of a boolean expression to select one of
    11→two values.
    12→
    13→> **Hands-on:** Try the [Create Dynamic Expressions](/terraform/tutorials/configuration-language/expressions?utm_source=WEBSITE&utm_medium=WEB_IO&utm_offer=ARTICLE_PAGE&utm_content=DOCS) tutorial.
    14→
    15→## Syntax
    16→
    17→The syntax of a conditional expression is as follows:
    18→
    19→```hcl
    20→condition ? true_val : false_val
    21→```
    22→
    23→If `condition` is `true` then the result is `true_val`. If `condition` is
    24→`false` then the result is `false_val`.
    25→
    26→A common use of conditional expressions is to define defaults to replace
    27→invalid values:
    28→
    29→```hcl
    30→var.a != "" ? var.a : "default-a"
    31→```
    32→
    33→If `var.a` is an empty string then the result is `"default-a"`, but otherwise
    34→it is the actual value of `var.a`.
    35→
    36→## Conditions
    37→
    38→The condition can be any expression that resolves to a boolean value. This will
    39→usually be an expression that uses the equality, comparison, or logical
    40→operators.
    41→
    42→### Custom Condition Checks
    43→
    44→You can create conditions that produce custom error messages for several types of objects in a configuration. For example, you can add a condition to an input variable that checks whether incoming image IDs are formatted properly.
    45→
    46→Custom conditions can help capture assumptions, helping future maintainers understand the configuration design and intent. They also return useful information about errors earlier and in context, helping consumers more easily diagnose issues in their configurations.
    47→
    48→Refer to [Custom Condition Checks](/terraform/language/expressions/custom-conditions#input-variable-validation) for details.
    49→
    50→## Result Types
    51→
    52→The two result values may be of any type, but they must both
    53→be of the _same_ type so that Terraform can determine what type the whole
    54→conditional expression will return without knowing the condition value.
    55→
    56→If the two result expressions don't produce the same type then Terraform will
    57→attempt to find a type that they can both convert to, and make those
    58→conversions automatically if so.
    59→
    60→For example, the following expression is valid and will always return a string,
    61→because in Terraform all numbers can convert automatically to a string using
    62→decimal digits:
    63→
    64→```hcl
    65→var.example ? 12 : "hello"
    66→```
    67→
    68→Relying on this automatic conversion behavior can be confusing for those who
    69→are not familiar with Terraform's conversion rules though, so we recommend
    70→being explicit using type conversion functions in any situation where there may
    71→be some uncertainty about the expected result type.
    72→
    73→The following example is contrived because it would be easier to write the
    74→constant `"12"` instead of the type conversion in this case, but shows how to
    75→use [`tostring`](/terraform/language/functions/tostring) to explicitly convert a number to
    76→a string.
    77→
    78→```hcl
    79→var.example ? tostring(12) : "hello"
    80→```
    81→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

[metadata] {"type": "text", "file": {"filePath": "/workspace/terraform-v1.10.0/website/docs/language/expressions/conditionals.mdx", "content": "---\npage_title: Conditional Expressions - Configuration Language\ndescription: >-\n  Conditional expressions select one of two values. You can use them to define\n  defaults to replace invalid values.\n---\n\n# Conditional Expressions\n\nA _conditional expression_ uses the value of a boolean expression to select one of\ntwo values.\n\n> **Hands-on:** Try the [Create Dynamic Expressions](/terraform/tutorials/configuration-language/expressions?utm_source=WEBSITE&utm_medium=WEB_IO&utm_offer=ARTICLE_PAGE&utm_content=DOCS) tutorial.\n\n## Syntax\n\nThe syntax of a conditional expression is as follows:\n\n```hcl\ncondition ? true_val : false_val\n```\n\nIf `condition` is `true` then the result is `true_val`. If `condition` is\n`false` then the result is `false_val`.\n\nA common use of conditional expressions is to define defaults to replace\ninvalid values:\n\n```hcl\nvar.a != \"\" ? var.a : \"default-a\"\n```\n\nIf `var.a` is an empty string then the result is `\"default-a\"`, but otherwise\nit is the actual value of `var.a`.\n\n## Conditions\n\nThe condition can be any expression that resolves to a boolean value. This will\nusually be an expression that uses the equality, comparison, or logical\noperators.\n\n### Custom Condition Checks\n\nYou can create conditions that produce custom error messages for several types of objects in a configuration. For example, you can add a condition to an input variable that checks whether incoming image IDs are formatted properly.\n\nCustom conditions can help capture assumptions, helping future maintainers understand the configuration design and intent. They also return useful information about errors earlier and in context, helping consumers more easily diagnose issues in their configurations.\n\nRefer to [Custom Condition Checks](/terraform/language/expressions/custom-conditions#input-variable-validation) for details.\n\n## Result Types\n\nThe two result values may be of any type, but they must both\nbe of the _same_ type so that Terraform can determine what type the whole\nconditional expression will return without knowing the condition value.\n\nIf the two result expressions don't produce the same type then Terraform will\nattempt to find a type that they can both convert to, and make those\nconversions automatically if so.\n\nFor example, the following expression is valid and will always return a string,\nbecause in Terraform all numbers can convert automatically to a string using\ndecimal digits:\n\n```hcl\nvar.example ? 12 : \"hello\"\n```\n\nRelying on this automatic conversion behavior can be confusing for those who\nare not familiar with Terraform's conversion rules though, so we recommend\nbeing explicit using type conversion functions in any situation where there may\nbe some uncertainty about the expected result type.\n\nThe following example is contrived because it would be easier to write the\nconstant `\"12\"` instead of the type conversion in this case, but shows how to\nuse [`tostring`](/terraform/language/functions/tostring) to explicitly convert a number to\na string.\n\n```hcl\nvar.example ? tostring(12) : \"hello\"\n```\n", "numLines": 81, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 81}}

arguments.prompt: Get the full changelog content for Terraform v1.10.0, especially all the bug fixes, new features, breaking changes, and notes about sensitive values, marks propagation, and conditional expressions

input.prompt: Get the full changelog content for Terraform v1.10.0, especially all the bug fixes, new features, breaking changes, and notes about sensitive values, marks propagation, and conditional expressions

tool_events.output:
# Terraform v1.10.0 Changelog Summary

## New Features

**Ephemeral Resources & Values**: The release introduces ephemeral resources that "are read anew during each phase of Terraform evaluation, and cannot be persisted to state storage." Ephemeral input variables and outputs are supported, along with a new `ephemeralasnull` function for converting ephemeral values to null.

## Key Bug Fixes

Notable corrections include:
- Kubernetes backend `secret_suffix` validation improvements
- Resolution of sensitive mark transfer issues causing incorrect plan changes
- Fixed GitHub module source ref handling with unencoded slashes
- Postcondition evaluation errors with multiple resource instances
- Invalid date generation in the `planstimestamp()` function
- S3 backend tag handling preferences

## Breaking Changes (Upgrade Notes)

- S3 backend removes deprecated IAM role assumption attributes; must use `assume_role` block
- S3 backend introduces native state locking; DynamoDB-based locking will eventually deprecate
- `moved` blocks now respect reserved keywords in resource address parsing
- Conditional expressions and for-expressions now consistently propagate marks, potentially showing additional sensitive results

## Enhancements

Performance improvements for resource evaluation with large instance counts, negative index support in the `element` function, and enhanced import block validation during `terraform validate`.

tool_events.output:
# Terraform v1.10.0 Release Summary

## Major Features

**Ephemeral Resources & Values**: Terraform now supports ephemeral resources that are "read anew during each phase of Terraform evaluation, and cannot be persisted to state storage." This includes a new `ephemeralasnull` function for converting ephemeral values to null.

## Critical Bug Fixes

Notable corrections address several state management issues:

- Sensitive marks were "incorrectly transferred to nested resource values, causing erroneous changes during a plan"
- Refresh-only plans with output changes are now properly applicable
- The `plantimestamp()` function validation now returns valid dates
- Conditional expressions and for loops no longer lose marks with certain unknown value combinations

## Backend Updates

**S3 Backend**: The service now supports native S3 state locking alongside existing DynamoDB mechanisms. Deprecated IAM role assumption attributes were removed in favor of the `assume_role` block.

**Kubernetes Backend**: Added validation for `secret_suffix` to prevent errors when suffixes end with numerals.

## Configuration & Syntax Changes

Moved blocks now respect reserved keywords, requiring "resource." prefixes for resources with type names matching top-level block keywords.

## Performance Improvements

Enhanced resource evaluation efficiency, particularly for configurations managing large numbers of resource instances.

Deprecated IAM role assumption attributes were removed in favor of the `assume_role` block.\n\n**Kubernetes Backend**: Added validation for `secret_suffix` to prevent errors when suffixes end with numerals.\n\n## Configuration & Syntax Changes\n\nMoved blocks now respect reserved keywords, requiring \"resource.\" prefixes for resources with type names matching top-level block keywords.\n\n## Performance Improvements\n\nEnhanced resource evaluation efficiency, particularly for configurations managing large numbers of resource instances.", "durationMs": 5493, "url": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/hashicorp/terraform/v1.10.0/CHANGELOG.md"}

tool_events.output:
# Terraform v1.10.0 Release Summary

**Release Date:** November 27, 2024

## Major Features

The release introduces two significant capabilities:

**Ephemeral Resources & Values:** Resources can now be marked as ephemeral, meaning they're "read anew during each phase of Terraform evaluation, and cannot be persisted to state storage." This prevents storing temporary or sensitive data in state files.

**New Function:** The `ephemeralasnull` function enables developers to "replace any ephemeral values with non-ephemeral null values" while preserving other data.

## Key Improvements

Multiple bug fixes address critical issues: sensitive marks being incorrectly transferred to nested resources, invalid date returns from `plantimestamp()`, and GitHub module source references being truncated.

Performance enhancements target "resource evaluation, especially when large numbers of resource instances are involved."

## Notable Changes

The S3 backend now supports native state locking alongside existing DynamoDB support. The `moved` block syntax now requires the `resource.` prefix when referencing resources with reserved keyword type names. Additionally, the `-state` flag is now deprecated across plan, apply, and refresh commands.

The `moved` block syntax now requires the `resource.` prefix when referencing resources with reserved keyword type names. Additionally, the `-state` flag is now deprecated across plan, apply, and refresh commands.", "durationMs": 5316, "url": "https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform/releases/tag/v1.10.0"}

1→---
     2→page_title: 'Backend Type: s3'
     3→description: Terraform can store state remotely in S3 and lock that state with DynamoDB.
     4→---
     5→
     6→# S3
     7→
     8→Stores the state as a given key in a given bucket on [Amazon S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/).
     9→This backend also supports state locking and consistency checking via [Dynamo DB](https://aws.amazon.com/dynamodb/), which can be enabled by setting the `dynamodb_table` field to an existing DynamoDB table name.
    10→A single DynamoDB table can be used to lock multiple remote state files.
    11→Terraform generates key names that include the values of the `bucket` and `key` variables.
    12→
    13→~> **Warning!** It is highly recommended that you enable
    14→[Bucket Versioning](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/manage-versioning-examples.html)
    15→on the S3 bucket to allow for state recovery in the case of accidental deletions and human error.
    16→
    17→## Example Configuration
    18→
    19→```hcl
    20→terraform {
    21→  backend "s3" {
    22→    bucket = "example-bucket"
    23→    key    = "path/to/state"
    24→    region = "us-east-1"
    25→  }
    26→}
    27→```
    28→
    29→This assumes we have a bucket created called `mybucket`. The Terraform state is written to the key `path/to/my/key`.
    30→
    31→Note that for the access credentials we recommend using a [partial configuration](/terraform/language/backend#partial-configuration).
    32→
    33→## State Storage
    34→
    35→The S3 backend stores state data in an S3 object at the path set by the `key` parameter in the S3 bucket indicated by the `bucket` parameter.
    36→Using the example shown above, the state would be stored at the path `path/to/my/key` in the bucket `mybucket`.
    37→
    38→When using [workspaces](/terraform/language/state/workspaces), the state for the `default` workspace is stored at the location described above.
    39→Other workspaces are stored using the path `<workspace_key_prefix>/<workspace_name>/<key>`.
    40→The default workspace key prefix is `env:` and it can be configured using the parameter `workspace_key_prefix`.
    41→Using the example above, the state for the workspace `development` would be stored at the path `env:/development/path/to/my/key`.
    42→
    43→## Permissions Required
    44→
    45→### S3 Bucket Permissions
    46→
    47→When not using [workspaces](/terraform/language/state/workspaces)(or when only using the `default` workspace), Terraform will need the following AWS IAM permissions on the target backend bucket:
    48→
    49→* `s3:ListBucket` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket`. At a minimum, this must be able to list the path where the state is stored.
    50→* `s3:GetObject` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/path/to/my/key`
    51→* `s3:PutObject` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/path/to/my/key`
    52→
    53→Note: `s3:DeleteObject` is not needed, as Terraform will not delete the state storage.
    54→
    55→This is seen in the following AWS IAM Statement:
    56→
    57→```json
    58→{
    59→  "Version": "2012-10-17",
    60→  "Statement": [
    61→    {
    62→      "Effect": "Allow",
    63→      "Action": "s3:ListBucket",
    64→      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::mybucket"
    65→    },
    66→    {
    67→      "Effect": "Allow",
    68→      "Action": ["s3:GetObject", "s3:PutObject"],
    69→      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/path/to/my/key"
    70→    }
    71→  ]
    72→}
    73→```
    74→
    75→When using [workspaces](/terraform/language/state/workspaces), Terraform will also need permissions to create, list, read, update, and delete the workspace state storage:
    76→
    77→* `s3:ListBucket` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket`. At a minumum, this must be able to list the path where the `default` workspace is stored as well as the other workspaces.
    78→* `s3:GetObject` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/path/to/my/key` and `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/<workspace_key_prefix>/*/path/to/my/key`
    79→* `s3:PutObject` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/path/to/my/key` and `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/<workspace_key_prefix>/*/path/to/my/key`
    80→* `s3:DeleteObject` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/<workspace_key_prefix>/*/path/to/my/key`
    81→
    82→-> **Note:** AWS can control access to S3 buckets with either IAM policies
    83→attached to users/groups/roles (like the example above) or resource policies
    84→attached to bucket objects (which look similar but also require a `Principal` to
    85→indicate which entity has those permissions). For more details, see Amazon's
    86→documentation about
    87→[S3 access control](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/s3-access-control.html).
    88→
    89→### DynamoDB Table Permissions
    90→
    91→If you are using state locking, Terraform will need the following AWS IAM
    92→permissions on the DynamoDB table (`arn:aws:dynamodb:::table/mytable`):
    93→
    94→* `dynamodb:DescribeTable`
    95→* `dynamodb:GetItem`
    96→* `dynamodb:PutItem`
    97→* `dynamodb:DeleteItem`
    98→
    99→This is seen in the following AWS IAM Statement:
   100→
