[metadata] {"type": "text", "file": {"filePath": "/workspace/terraform-v1.10.0/website/docs/language/backend/s3.mdx", "content": "---\npage_title: 'Backend Type: s3'\ndescription: Terraform can store state remotely in S3 and lock that state with DynamoDB.\n---\n\n# S3\n\nStores the state as a given key in a given bucket on [Amazon S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/).\nThis backend also supports state locking and consistency checking via [Dynamo DB](https://aws.amazon.com/dynamodb/), which can be enabled by setting the `dynamodb_table` field to an existing DynamoDB table name.\nA single DynamoDB table can be used to lock multiple remote state files.\nTerraform generates key names that include the values of the `bucket` and `key` variables.\n\n~> **Warning!** It is highly recommended that you enable\n[Bucket Versioning](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/manage-versioning-examples.html)\non the S3 bucket to allow for state recovery in the case of accidental deletions and human error.\n\n## Example Configuration\n\n```hcl\nterraform {\n  backend \"s3\" {\n    bucket = \"example-bucket\"\n    key    = \"path/to/state\"\n    region = \"us-east-1\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\nThis assumes we have a bucket created called `mybucket`. The Terraform state is written to the key `path/to/my/key`.\n\nNote that for the access credentials we recommend using a [partial configuration](/terraform/language/backend#partial-configuration).\n\n## State Storage\n\nThe S3 backend stores state data in an S3 object at the path set by the `key` parameter in the S3 bucket indicated by the `bucket` parameter.\nUsing the example shown above, the state would be stored at the path `path/to/my/key` in the bucket `mybucket`.\n\nWhen using [workspaces](/terraform/language/state/workspaces), the state for the `default` workspace is stored at the location described above.\nOther workspaces are stored using the path `<workspace_key_prefix>/<workspace_name>/<key>`.\nThe default workspace key prefix is `env:` and it can be configured using the parameter `workspace_key_prefix`.\nUsing the example above, the state for the workspace `development` would be stored at the path `env:/development/path/to/my/key`.\n\n## Permissions Required\n\n### S3 Bucket Permissions\n\nWhen not using [workspaces](/terraform/language/state/workspaces)(or when only using the `default` workspace), Terraform will need the following AWS IAM permissions on the target backend bucket:\n\n* `s3:ListBucket` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket`. At a minimum, this must be able to list the path where the state is stored.\n* `s3:GetObject` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/path/to/my/key`\n* `s3:PutObject` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/path/to/my/key`\n\nNote: `s3:DeleteObject` is not needed, as Terraform will not delete the state storage.\n\nThis is seen in the following AWS IAM Statement:\n\n```json\n{\n  \"Version\": \"2012-10-17\",\n  \"Statement\": [\n    {\n      \"Effect\": \"Allow\",\n      \"Action\": \"s3:ListBucket\",\n      \"Resource\": \"arn:aws:s3:::mybucket\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"Effect\": \"Allow\",\n      \"Action\": [\"s3:GetObject\", \"s3:PutObject\"],\n      \"Resource\": \"arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/path/to/my/key\"\n    }\n  ]\n}\n```\n\nWhen using [workspaces](/terraform/language/state/workspaces), Terraform will also need permissions to create, list, read, update, and delete the workspace state storage:\n\n* `s3:ListBucket` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket`. At a minumum, this must be able to list the path where the `default` workspace is stored as well as the other workspaces.\n* `s3:GetObject` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/path/to/my/key` and `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/<workspace_key_prefix>/*/path/to/my/key`\n* `s3:PutObject` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/path/to/my/key` and `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/<workspace_key_prefix>/*/path/to/my/key`\n* `s3:DeleteObject` on `arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/<workspace_key_prefix>/*/path/to/my/key`\n\n-> **Note:** AWS can control access to S3 buckets with either IAM policies\nattached to users/groups/roles (like the example above) or resource policies\nattached to bucket objects (which look similar but also require a `Principal` to\nindicate which entity has those permissions). For more details, see Amazon's\ndocumentation about\n[S3 access control](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/s3-access-control.html).\n\n### DynamoDB Table Permissions\n\nIf you are using state locking, Terraform will need the following AWS IAM\npermissions on the DynamoDB table (`arn:aws:dynamodb:::table/mytable`):\n\n* `dynamodb:DescribeTable`\n* `dynamodb:GetItem`\n* `dynamodb:PutItem`\n* `dynamodb:DeleteItem`\n\nThis is seen in the following AWS IAM Statement:\n", "numLines": 100, "startLine": 1, "totalLines": 634}}

100→
   101→```json
   102→{
   103→  "Version": "2012-10-17",
   104→  "Statement": [
   105→    {
   106→      "Effect": "Allow",
   107→      "Action": [
   108→        "dynamodb:DescribeTable",
   109→        "dynamodb:GetItem",
   110→        "dynamodb:PutItem",
   111→        "dynamodb:DeleteItem"
   112→      ],
   113→      "Resource": "arn:aws:dynamodb:*:*:table/mytable"
   114→    }
   115→  ]
   116→}
   117→```
   118→
   119→## Data Source Configuration
   120→
   121→To make use of the S3 remote state in another configuration,
   122→use the [`terraform_remote_state` data source](/terraform/language/state/remote-state-data).
   123→
   124→```hcl
   125→data "terraform_remote_state" "network" {
   126→  backend = "s3"
   127→  config = {
   128→    bucket = "terraform-state-prod"
   129→    key    = "network/terraform.tfstate"
   130→    region = "us-east-1"
   131→  }
   132→}
   133→```
   134→
   135→The `terraform_remote_state` data source will return all of the root module
   136→outputs defined in the referenced remote state (but not any outputs from
   137→nested modules unless they are explicitly output again in the root). An
   138→example output might look like:
   139→
   140→```
   141→data.terraform_remote_state.network:
   142→  id = 2016-10-29 01:57:59.780010914 +0000 UTC
   143→  addresses.# = 2
   144→  addresses.0 = 52.207.220.222
   145→  addresses.1 = 54.196.78.166
   146→  backend = s3
   147→  config.% = 3
   148→  config.bucket = terraform-state-prod
   149→  config.key = network/terraform.tfstate
   150→  config.region = us-east-1
   151→  elb_address = web-elb-790251200.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com
   152→  public_subnet_id = subnet-1e05dd33
   153→```
   154→
   155→## Configuration
   156→
   157→This backend requires the configuration of the AWS Region and S3 state storage. Other configuration, such as enabling DynamoDB state locking, is optional.
   158→
   159→### Credentials and Shared Configuration
   160→
   161→!> **Warning:**  We recommend using environment variables to supply credentials and other sensitive data. If you use `-backend-config` or hardcode these values directly in your configuration, Terraform will include these values in both the `.terraform` subdirectory and in plan files. Refer to [Credentials and Sensitive Data](/terraform/language/backend#credentials-and-sensitive-data) for details.
   162→
   163→The following configuration is required:
   164→
   165→* `region` - (Required) AWS Region of the S3 Bucket and DynamoDB Table (if used). This can also be sourced from the `AWS_DEFAULT_REGION` and `AWS_REGION` environment variables.
   166→
   167→The following configuration is optional:
   168→
   169→* `use_lockfile` - (Experimental, Optional) Whether to use a lockfile for locking the state file. Defaults to `false`.
   170→* `access_key` - (Optional) AWS access key. If configured, must also configure `secret_key`. This can also be sourced from the `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID` environment variable, AWS shared credentials file (e.g. `~/.aws/credentials`), or AWS shared configuration file (e.g. `~/.aws/config`).
   171→* `allowed_account_ids` - (Optional) List of allowed AWS account IDs to prevent potential destruction of a live environment. Conflicts with `forbidden_account_ids`.
   172→* `custom_ca_bundle` - (Optional) File containing custom root and intermediate certificates. Can also be set using the `AWS_CA_BUNDLE` environment variable. Setting ca_bundle in the shared config file is not supported.
   173→* `ec2_metadata_service_endpoint` - (Optional) Custom endpoint URL for the EC2 Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) API.
   174→  Can also be set with the `AWS_EC2_METADATA_SERVICE_ENDPOINT` environment variable.
   175→* `ec2_metadata_service_endpoint_mode` - (Optional) Mode to use in communicating with the metadata service.
   176→  Valid values are `IPv4` and `IPv6`.
   177→  Can also be set with the `AWS_EC2_METADATA_SERVICE_ENDPOINT_MODE` environment variable.
   178→* `forbidden_account_ids` - (Optional) List of forbidden AWS account IDs to prevent potential destruction of a live environment. Conflicts with `allowed_account_ids`.
   179→* `http_proxy` - (Optional) URL of a proxy to use for HTTP requests when accessing the AWS API.
   180→  Can also be set using the `HTTP_PROXY` or `http_proxy` environment variables.
   181→* `https_proxy` - (Optional) URL of a proxy to use for HTTPS requests when accessing the AWS API.
   182→  Can also be set using the `HTTPS_PROXY` or `https_proxy` environment variables.
   183→* `iam_endpoint` - (Optional, **Deprecated**) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) API.
   184→  Use `endpoints.iam` instead.
   185→* `insecure` - (Optional) Whether to explicitly allow the backend to perform "insecure" SSL requests. If omitted, the default value is `false`.
   186→* `no_proxy` - (Optional) Comma-separated list of hosts that should not use HTTP or HTTPS proxies.
   187→  Each value can be one of:
   188→    * A domain name
   189→    * An IP address
   190→    * A CIDR address
   191→    * An asterisk (`*`), to indicate that no proxying should be performed
   192→  Domain name and IP address values can also include a port number.
   193→  Can also be set using the `NO_PROXY` or `no_proxy` environment variables.
   194→* `max_retries` - (Optional) The maximum number of times an AWS API request is retried on retryable failure. Defaults to 5.
   195→* `profile` - (Optional) Name of AWS profile in AWS shared credentials file (e.g. `~/.aws/credentials`) or AWS shared configuration file (e.g. `~/.aws/config`) to use for credentials and/or configuration. This can also be sourced from the `AWS_PROFILE` environment variable.
   196→* `retry_mode` - (Optional) Specifies how retries are attempted. Valid values are `standard` and `adaptive`. Can also be configured using the `AWS_RETRY_MODE` environment variable or the shared config file parameter `retry_mode`.
   197→* `secret_key` - (Optional) AWS access key. If configured, must also configure `access_key`. This can also be sourced from the `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` environment variable, AWS shared credentials file (e.g. `~/.aws/credentials`), or AWS shared configuration file (e.g. `~/.aws/config`).
   198→* `shared_config_files`  - (Optional) List of paths to AWS shared configuration files. Defaults to `~/.aws/config`.
   199→* `shared_credentials_file`  - (Optional, **Deprecated**, use `shared_credentials_files` instead) Path to the AWS shared credentials file. Defaults to `~/.aws/credentials`.
   200→* `shared_credentials_files`  - (Optional) List of paths to AWS shared credentials files. Defaults to `~/.aws/credentials`.
   201→* `skip_credentials_validation` - (Optional) Skip credentials validation via the STS API.
   202→  Useful for testing and for AWS API implementations that do not have STS available.
   203→* `skip_region_validation` - (Optional) Skip validation of provided region name.
   204→* `skip_requesting_account_id` - (Optional) Whether to skip requesting the account ID.
   205→  Useful for AWS API implementations that do not have the IAM, STS API, or metadata API.
   206→* `skip_metadata_api_check` - (Optional) Skip usage of EC2 Metadata API.
   207→* `skip_s3_checksum` - (Optional) Do not include checksum when uploading S3 Objects.
   208→  Useful for some S3-Compatible APIs.
   209→* `sts_endpoint` - (Optional, **Deprecated**) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS Security Token Service (STS) API.
   210→  Use `endpoints.sts` instead.
   211→* `sts_region` - (Optional) AWS region for STS. If unset, AWS will use the same region for STS as other non-STS operations.
   212→* `token` - (Optional) Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) token. This can also be sourced from the `AWS_SESSION_TOKEN` environment variable.
   213→* `use_dualstack_endpoint` - (Optional) Force the backend to resolve endpoints with DualStack capability. Can also be set with the `AWS_USE_DUALSTACK_ENDPOINT` environment variable or in a shared config file (`use_dualstack_endpoint`).
   214→* `use_fips_endpoint` - (Optional) Force the backend to resolve endpoints with FIPS capability. Can also be set with the `AWS_USE_FIPS_ENDPOINT` environment variable or in a shared config file (`use_fips_endpoint`).
   215→
   216→#### Overriding AWS API endpoints
   217→
   218→The optional argument `endpoints` contains the following arguments:
   219→
   220→* `dynamodb` - (Optional) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS DynamoDB API.
   221→  This can also be sourced from the environment variable `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL_DYNAMODB` or the deprecated environment variable `AWS_DYNAMODB_ENDPOINT`.
   222→* `iam` - (Optional) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS IAM API.
   223→  This can also be sourced from the environment variable `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL_IAM` or the deprecated environment variable `AWS_IAM_ENDPOINT`.
   224→* `s3` - (Optional) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS S3 API.
   225→  This can also be sourced from the environment variable `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL_S3` or the deprecated environment variable `AWS_S3_ENDPOINT`.
   226→* `sso` - (Optional) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly known as AWS SSO) API.
   227→  This can also be sourced from the environment variable `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL_SSO`.
   228→* `sts` - (Optional) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS STS API.
   229→  This can also be sourced from the environment variable `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL_STS` or the deprecated environment variable `AWS_STS_ENDPOINT`.
   230→
   231→The environment variable `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL` can be used to set a base endpoint URL for all services.
   232→
   233→Endpoints can also be overridden using the [AWS shared configuration file](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sdkref/latest/guide/feature-ss-endpoints.html#ss-endpoints-config).
   234→Setting the parameter `endpoint_url` on a profile will set that endpoint for all services.
   235→To set endpoints for specific services, create a `services` section and set the `endpoint_url` parameters for each desired service.
   236→Endpoints set for specific services will override the base endpoint configured in the profile.
   237→
   238→#### Assume Role Configuration
   239→
   240→The argument `assume_role` contains the following arguments:
   241→
   242→* `role_arn` - (Required) Amazon Resource Name (ARN) of the IAM Role to assume.
   243→* `duration` - (Optional) The duration individual credentials will be valid.
   244→  Credentials are automatically renewed up to the maximum defined by the AWS account.
   245→  Specified using the format `<hours>h<minutes>m<seconds>s` with any unit being optional.
   246→  For example, an hour and a half can be specified as `1h30m` or `90m`. 
   247→  Must be between 15 minutes (15m) and 12 hours (12h).
   248→* `external_id` - (Optional) External identifier to use when assuming the role.
   249→* `policy` - (Optional) IAM Policy JSON describing further restricting permissions for the IAM Role being assumed.
   250→* `policy_arns` - (Optional) Set of Amazon Resource Names (ARNs) of IAM Policies describing further restricting permissions for the IAM Role being assumed.
   251→* `session_name` - (Optional) Session name to use when assuming the role.
   252→* `source_identity` - (Optional) Source identity specified by the principal assuming the role.
   253→* `tags` - (Optional) Map of assume role session tags.
   254→* `transitive_tag_keys` - (Optional) Set of assume role session tag keys to pass to any subsequent sessions.
   255→
   256→Multiple `assume_role` values can be specified, and the roles will be assumed in order.
   257→
   258→```terraform
   259→terraform {
   260→  backend "s3" {
   261→    bucket = "example-bucket"
   262→    key    = "path/to/state"
   263→    region = "us-east-1"
   264→    assume_role = {
   265→      role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::PRODUCTION-ACCOUNT-ID:role/Terraform"
   266→    }
   267→  }
   268→}
   269→```
   270→
   271→#### Assume Role With Web Identity Configuration
   272→
   273→The following `assume_role_with_web_identity` configuration block is optional:
   274→
   275→* `role_arn` - (Required) Amazon Resource Name (ARN) of the IAM Role to assume.
   276→  Can also be set with the `AWS_ROLE_ARN` environment variable.
   277→* `duration` - (Optional) The duration individual credentials will be valid.
   278→  Credentials are automatically renewed up to the maximum defined by the AWS account.
   279→  Specified using the format `<hours>h<minutes>m<seconds>s` with any unit being optional.
   280→  For example, an hour and a half can be specified as `1h30m` or `90m`. 
   281→  Must be between 15 minutes (15m) and 12 hours (12h).
   282→* `policy` - (Optional) IAM Policy JSON describing further restricting permissions for the IAM Role being assumed.
   283→* `policy_arns` - (Optional) Set of Amazon Resource Names (ARNs) of IAM Policies describing further restricting permissions for the IAM Role being assumed.
   284→* `session_name` - (Optional) Session name to use when assuming the role.
   285→  Can also be set with the `AWS_ROLE_SESSION_NAME` environment variable.
   286→* `web_identity_token` - (Optional) The value of a web identity token from an OpenID Connect (OIDC) or OAuth provider.
   287→  One of `web_identity_token` or `web_identity_token_file` is required.
   288→* `web_identity_token_file` - (Optional) File containing a web identity token from an OpenID Connect (OIDC) or OAuth provider.
   289→  One of `web_identity_token_file` or `web_identity_token` is required.
   290→  Can also be set with the `AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE` environment variable.
   291→
   292→```terraform
   293→terraform {
   294→  backend "s3" {
   295→    bucket = "example-bucket"
   296→    key    = "path/to/state"
   297→    region = "us-east-1"
   298→    assume_role_with_web_identity = {
   299→      role_arn           = "arn:aws:iam::PRODUCTION-ACCOUNT-ID:role/Terraform"

If you use `-backend-config` or hardcode these values directly in your configuration, Terraform will include these values in both the `.terraform` subdirectory and in plan files. Refer to [Credentials and Sensitive Data](/terraform/language/backend#credentials-and-sensitive-data) for details.\n\nThe following configuration is required:\n\n* `region` - (Required) AWS Region of the S3 Bucket and DynamoDB Table (if used). This can also be sourced from the `AWS_DEFAULT_REGION` and `AWS_REGION` environment variables.\n\nThe following configuration is optional:\n\n* `use_lockfile` - (Experimental, Optional) Whether to use a lockfile for locking the state file. This can also be sourced from the `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID` environment variable, AWS shared credentials file (e.g. `~/.aws/credentials`), or AWS shared configuration file (e.g. `~/.aws/config`).\n* `allowed_account_ids` - (Optional) List of allowed AWS account IDs to prevent potential destruction of a live environment. Setting ca_bundle in the shared config file is not supported.\n* `ec2_metadata_service_endpoint` - (Optional) Custom endpoint URL for the EC2 Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) API.\n  Can also be set with the `AWS_EC2_METADATA_SERVICE_ENDPOINT` environment variable.\n* `ec2_metadata_service_endpoint_mode` - (Optional) Mode to use in communicating with the metadata service.\n  Valid values are `IPv4` and `IPv6`.\n  Can also be set with the `AWS_EC2_METADATA_SERVICE_ENDPOINT_MODE` environment variable.\n* `forbidden_account_ids` - (Optional) List of forbidden AWS account IDs to prevent potential destruction of a live environment. Conflicts with `allowed_account_ids`.\n* `http_proxy` - (Optional) URL of a proxy to use for HTTP requests when accessing the AWS API.\n  Can also be set using the `HTTP_PROXY` or `http_proxy` environment variables.\n* `https_proxy` - (Optional) URL of a proxy to use for HTTPS requests when accessing the AWS API.\n  Can also be set using the `HTTPS_PROXY` or `https_proxy` environment variables.\n* `iam_endpoint` - (Optional, **Deprecated**) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) API.\n  Use `endpoints.iam` instead.\n* `insecure` - (Optional) Whether to explicitly allow the backend to perform \"insecure\" SSL requests. If omitted, the default value is `false`.\n* `no_proxy` - (Optional) Comma-separated list of hosts that should not use HTTP or HTTPS proxies.\n  Each value can be one of:\n    * A domain name\n    * An IP address\n    * A CIDR address\n    * An asterisk (`*`), to indicate that no proxying should be performed\n  Domain name and IP address values can also include a port number.\n  Can also be set using the `NO_PROXY` or `no_proxy` environment variables.\n* `max_retries` - (Optional) The maximum number of times an AWS API request is retried on retryable failure. Defaults to 5.\n* `profile` - (Optional) Name of AWS profile in AWS shared credentials file (e.g. `~/.aws/credentials`) or AWS shared configuration file (e.g. `~/.aws/config`) to use for credentials and/or configuration. This can also be sourced from the `AWS_PROFILE` environment variable.\n* `retry_mode` - (Optional) Specifies how retries are attempted. Valid values are `standard` and `adaptive`. Can also be configured using the `AWS_RETRY_MODE` environment variable or the shared config file parameter `retry_mode`.\n* `secret_key` - (Optional) AWS access key. If configured, must also configure `access_key`. This can also be sourced from the `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` environment variable, AWS shared credentials file (e.g. `~/.aws/credentials`), or AWS shared configuration file (e.g. `~/.aws/config`).\n* `shared_config_files`  - (Optional) List of paths to AWS shared configuration files. Defaults to `~/.aws/config`.\n* `shared_credentials_file`  - (Optional, **Deprecated**, use `shared_credentials_files` instead) Path to the AWS shared credentials file. Defaults to `~/.aws/credentials`.\n* `shared_credentials_files`  - (Optional) List of paths to AWS shared credentials files. Defaults to `~/.aws/credentials`.\n* `skip_credentials_validation` - (Optional) Skip credentials validation via the STS API.\n  Useful for testing and for AWS API implementations that do not have STS available.\n* `skip_region_validation` - (Optional) Skip validation of provided region name.\n* `skip_requesting_account_id` - (Optional) Whether to skip requesting the account ID.\n  Useful for AWS API implementations that do not have the IAM, STS API, or metadata API.\n* `skip_metadata_api_check` - (Optional) Skip usage of EC2 Metadata API.\n* `skip_s3_checksum` - (Optional) Do not include checksum when uploading S3 Objects.\n  Useful for some S3-Compatible APIs.\n* `sts_endpoint` - (Optional, **Deprecated**) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS Security Token Service (STS) API.\n  Use `endpoints.sts` instead.\n* `sts_region` - (Optional) AWS region for STS. If unset, AWS will use the same region for STS as other non-STS operations.\n* `token` - (Optional) Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) token. This can also be sourced from the `AWS_SESSION_TOKEN` environment variable.\n* `use_dualstack_endpoint` - (Optional) Force the backend to resolve endpoints with DualStack capability. Can also be set with the `AWS_USE_DUALSTACK_ENDPOINT` environment variable or in a shared config file (`use_dualstack_endpoint`).\n* `use_fips_endpoint` - (Optional) Force the backend to resolve endpoints with FIPS capability. Can also be set with the `AWS_USE_FIPS_ENDPOINT` environment variable or in a shared config file (`use_fips_endpoint`).\n\n#### Overriding AWS API endpoints\n\nThe optional argument `endpoints` contains the following arguments:\n\n* `dynamodb` - (Optional) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS DynamoDB API.\n  This can also be sourced from the environment variable `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL_DYNAMODB` or the deprecated environment variable `AWS_DYNAMODB_ENDPOINT`.\n* `iam` - (Optional) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS IAM API.\n  This can also be sourced from the environment variable `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL_IAM` or the deprecated environment variable `AWS_IAM_ENDPOINT`.\n* `s3` - (Optional) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS S3 API.\n  This can also be sourced from the environment variable `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL_S3` or the deprecated environment variable `AWS_S3_ENDPOINT`.\n* `sso` - (Optional) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly known as AWS SSO) API.\n  This can also be sourced from the environment variable `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL_SSO`.\n* `sts` - (Optional) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS STS API.\n  This can also be sourced from the environment variable `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL_STS` or the deprecated environment variable `AWS_STS_ENDPOINT`.\n\nThe environment variable `AWS_ENDPOINT_URL` can be used to set a base endpoint URL for all services.\n\nEndpoints can also be overridden using the [AWS shared configuration file](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sdkref/latest/guide/feature-ss-endpoints.html#ss-endpoints-config).\nSetting the parameter `endpoint_url` on a profile will set that endpoint for all services.\nTo set endpoints for specific services, create a `services` section and set the `endpoint_url` parameters for each desired service.\nEndpoints set for specific services will override the base endpoint configured in the profile.\n\n#### Assume Role Configuration\n\nThe argument `assume_role` contains the following arguments:\n\n* `role_arn` - (Required) Amazon Resource Name (ARN) of the IAM Role to assume.\n* `duration` - (Optional) The duration individual credentials will be valid.\n  Credentials are automatically renewed up to the maximum defined by the AWS account.\n  Specified using the format `<hours>h<minutes>m<seconds>s` with any unit being optional.\n  For example, an hour and a half can be specified as `1h30m` or `90m`. \n  Must be between 15 minutes (15m) and 12 hours (12h).\n* `external_id` - (Optional) External identifier to use when assuming the role.\n* `policy` - (Optional) IAM Policy JSON describing further restricting permissions for the IAM Role being assumed.\n* `policy_arns` - (Optional) Set of Amazon Resource Names (ARNs) of IAM Policies describing further restricting permissions for the IAM Role being assumed.\n* `session_name` - (Optional) Session name to use when assuming the role.\n* `source_identity` - (Optional) Source identity specified by the principal assuming the role.\n* `tags` - (Optional) Map of assume role session tags.\n* `transitive_tag_keys` - (Optional) Set of assume role session tag keys to pass to any subsequent sessions.\n\nMultiple `assume_role` values can be specified, and the roles will be assumed in order.\n\n```terraform\nterraform {\n  backend \"s3\" {\n    bucket = \"example-bucket\"\n    key    = \"path/to/state\"\n    region = \"us-east-1\"\n    assume_role = {\n      role_arn = \"arn:aws:iam::PRODUCTION-ACCOUNT-ID:role/Terraform\"\n    }\n  }\n}\n```\n\n#### Assume Role With Web Identity Configuration\n\nThe following `assume_role_with_web_identity` configuration block is optional:\n\n* `role_arn` - (Required) Amazon Resource Name (ARN) of the IAM Role to assume.\n  Can also be set with the `AWS_ROLE_ARN` environment variable.\n* `duration` - (Optional) The duration individual credentials will be valid.\n  Credentials are automatically renewed up to the maximum defined by the AWS account.\n  Specified using the format `<hours>h<minutes>m<seconds>s` with any unit being optional.\n  For example, an hour and a half can be specified as `1h30m` or `90m`. \n  Must be between 15 minutes (15m) and 12 hours (12h).\n* `policy` - (Optional) IAM Policy JSON describing further restricting permissions for the IAM Role being assumed.\n* `policy_arns` - (Optional) Set of Amazon Resource Names (ARNs) of IAM Policies describing further restricting permissions for the IAM Role being assumed.\n* `session_name` - (Optional) Session name to use when assuming the role.\n  Can also be set with the `AWS_ROLE_SESSION_NAME` environment variable.\n* `web_identity_token` - (Optional) The value of a web identity token from an OpenID Connect (OIDC) or OAuth provider.\n  One of `web_identity_token` or `web_identity_token_file` is required.\n* `web_identity_token_file` - (Optional) File containing a web identity token from an OpenID Connect (OIDC) or OAuth provider.\n  One of `web_identity_token_file` or `web_identity_token` is required.\n  Can also be set with the `AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE` environment variable.\n\n```terraform\nterraform {\n  backend \"s3\" {\n    bucket = \"example-bucket\"\n    key    = \"path/to/state\"\n    region = \"us-east-1\"\n    assume_role_with_web_identity = {\n      role_arn           = \"arn:aws:iam::PRODUCTION-ACCOUNT-ID:role/Terraform\"", "numLines": 200, "startLine": 100, "totalLines": 634}}

   305→
   306→### S3 State Storage
   307→
   308→The following configuration is required:
   309→
   310→* `bucket` - (Required) Name of the S3 Bucket.
   311→* `key` - (Required) Path to the state file inside the S3 Bucket. When using a non-default [workspace](/terraform/language/state/workspaces), the state path will be `<workspace_key_prefix>/<workspace_name>/<key>` (see also the `workspace_key_prefix` configuration).
   312→
   313→The following configuration is optional:
   314→
   315→* `acl` - (Optional) [Canned ACL](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/acl-overview.html#canned-acl) to be applied to the state and lock files.
   316→* `encrypt` - (Optional) Enable [server side encryption](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingServerSideEncryption.html) of the state and lock files.
   317→* `endpoint` - (Optional, **Deprecated**) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS S3 API.
   318→  Use `endpoints.s3` instead.
   319→* `force_path_style` - (Optional, **Deprecated**) Enable path-style S3 URLs (`https://<HOST>/<BUCKET>` instead of `https://<BUCKET>.<HOST>`).
   320→* `kms_key_id` - (Optional) Amazon Resource Name (ARN) of a Key Management Service (KMS) Key to use for encrypting the state and lock files. Note that if this value is specified, Terraform will need `kms:Encrypt`, `kms:Decrypt` and `kms:GenerateDataKey` permissions on this KMS key.
   321→* `sse_customer_key` - (Optional) The key to use for encrypting state and lock files with [Server-Side Encryption with Customer-Provided Keys (SSE-C)](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/ServerSideEncryptionCustomerKeys.html). This is the base64-encoded value of the key, which must decode to 256 bits. This can also be sourced from the `AWS_SSE_CUSTOMER_KEY` environment variable, which is recommended due to the sensitivity of the value. Setting it inside a terraform file will cause it to be persisted to disk in `terraform.tfstate`.
   322→* `use_path_style` - (Optional) Enable path-style S3 URLs (`https://<HOST>/<BUCKET>` instead of `https://<BUCKET>.<HOST>`).
   323→* `workspace_key_prefix` - (Optional) Prefix applied to the state path inside the bucket. This is only relevant when using a non-default workspace. Defaults to `env:`.
   324→
   325→### State Locking
   326→
   327→State locking is an opt-in feature of the S3 backend.
   328→
   329→Locking can be enabled via an S3 "lockfile" (introduced as **experimental** in Terraform 1.10) or DynamoDB.
   330→To support migration from older versions of Terraform which only support DynamoDB-based locking, the S3 and DynamoDB arguments below can be configured simultaneously.
   331→In a future minor version the DynamoDB locking mechanism will be removed.
   332→
   333→To enable S3 state locking, use the following optional argument:
   334→
   335→* `use_lockfile` - (Optional, Experimental) Whether to use a lockfile for locking the state file. Defaults to `false`.
   336→
   337→To enable DynamoDB state locking, use the following optional arguments:
   338→
   339→* `dynamodb_endpoint` - (Optional, **Deprecated**) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS DynamoDB API.
   340→  Use `endpoints.dynamodb` instead.
   341→* `dynamodb_table` - (Optional) Name of DynamoDB Table to use for state locking and consistency. The table must have a partition key named `LockID` with type of `String`.
   342→
   343→## Multi-account AWS Architecture
   344→
   345→A common architectural pattern is for an organization to use a number of
   346→separate AWS accounts to isolate different teams and environments. For example,
   347→a "staging" system will often be deployed into a separate AWS account than
   348→its corresponding "production" system, to minimize the risk of the staging
   349→environment affecting production infrastructure, whether via rate limiting,
   350→misconfigured access controls, or other unintended interactions.
   351→
   352→The S3 backend can be used in a number of different ways that make different
   353→tradeoffs between convenience, security, and isolation in such an organization.
   354→This section describes one such approach that aims to find a good compromise
   355→between these tradeoffs, allowing use of
   356→[Terraform's workspaces feature](/terraform/language/state/workspaces) to switch
   357→conveniently between multiple isolated deployments of the same configuration.
   358→
   359→Use this section as a starting-point for your approach, but note that
   360→you will probably need to make adjustments for the unique standards and
   361→regulations that apply to your organization. You will also need to make some
   362→adjustments to this approach to account for _existing_ practices within your
   363→organization, if for example other tools have previously been used to manage
   364→infrastructure.
   365→
   366→Terraform is an administrative tool that manages your infrastructure, and so
   367→ideally the infrastructure that is used by Terraform should exist outside of
   368→the infrastructure that Terraform manages. This can be achieved by creating a
   369→separate _administrative_ AWS account which contains the user accounts used by
   370→human operators and any infrastructure and tools used to manage the other
   371→accounts. Isolating shared administrative tools from your main environments
   372→has a number of advantages, such as avoiding accidentally damaging the
   373→administrative infrastructure while changing the target infrastructure, and
   374→reducing the risk that an attacker might abuse production infrastructure to
   375→gain access to the (usually more privileged) administrative infrastructure.
   376→
   377→### Administrative Account Setup
   378→
   379→Your administrative AWS account will contain at least the following items:
   380→
   381→* One or more [IAM user](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_users.html)
   382→  for system administrators that will log in to maintain infrastructure in
   383→  the other accounts.
   384→* Optionally, one or more [IAM groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_groups.html)
   385→  to differentiate between different groups of users that have different
   386→  levels of access to the other AWS accounts.
   387→* An [S3 bucket](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingBucket.html)
   388→  that will contain the Terraform state files for each workspace.
   389→* A [DynamoDB table](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/HowItWorks.CoreComponents.html#HowItWorks.CoreComponents.TablesItemsAttributes)
   390→  that will be used for locking to prevent concurrent operations on a single
   391→  workspace.
   392→
   393→Provide the S3 bucket name and DynamoDB table name to Terraform within the
   394→S3 backend configuration using the `bucket` and `dynamodb_table` arguments
   395→respectively, and configure a suitable `workspace_key_prefix` to contain
   396→the states of the various workspaces that will subsequently be created for
   397→this configuration.
   398→
   399→### Environment Account Setup

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

Defaults to `false`.\n\nTo enable DynamoDB state locking, use the following optional arguments:\n\n* `dynamodb_endpoint` - (Optional, **Deprecated**) Custom endpoint URL for the AWS DynamoDB API.\n  Use `endpoints.dynamodb` instead.\n* `dynamodb_table` - (Optional) Name of DynamoDB Table to use for state locking and consistency. The table must have a partition key named `LockID` with type of `String`.\n\n## Multi-account AWS Architecture\n\nA common architectural pattern is for an organization to use a number of\nseparate AWS accounts to isolate different teams and environments. For example,\na \"staging\" system will often be deployed into a separate AWS account than\nits corresponding \"production\" system, to minimize the risk of the staging\nenvironment affecting production infrastructure, whether via rate limiting,\nmisconfigured access controls, or other unintended interactions.\n\nThe S3 backend can be used in a number of different ways that make different\ntradeoffs between convenience, security, and isolation in such an organization.\nThis section describes one such approach that aims to find a good compromise\nbetween these tradeoffs, allowing use of\n[Terraform's workspaces feature](/terraform/language/state/workspaces) to switch\nconveniently between multiple isolated deployments of the same configuration.\n\nUse this section as a starting-point for your approach, but note that\nyou will probably need to make adjustments for the unique standards and\nregulations that apply to your organization. This can be achieved by creating a\nseparate _administrative_ AWS account which contains the user accounts used by\nhuman operators and any infrastructure and tools used to manage the other\naccounts. Isolating shared administrative tools from your main environments\nhas a number of advantages, such as avoiding accidentally damaging the\nadministrative infrastructure while changing the target infrastructure, and\nreducing the risk that an attacker might abuse production infrastructure to\ngain access to the (usually more privileged) administrative infrastructure.\n\n### Administrative Account Setup\n\nYour administrative AWS account will contain at least the following items:\n\n* One or more [IAM user](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_users.html)\n  for system administrators that will log in to maintain infrastructure in\n  the other accounts.\n* Optionally, one or more [IAM groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_groups.html)\n  to differentiate between different groups of users that have different\n  levels of access to the other AWS accounts.\n* An [S3 bucket](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/UsingBucket.html)\n  that will contain the Terraform state files for each workspace.\n* A [DynamoDB table](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/HowItWorks.CoreComponents.html#HowItWorks.CoreComponents.TablesItemsAttributes)\n  that will be used for locking to prevent concurrent operations on a single\n  workspace.\n\nProvide the S3 bucket name and DynamoDB table name to Terraform within the\nS3 backend configuration using the `bucket` and `dynamodb_table` arguments\nrespectively, and configure a suitable `workspace_key_prefix` to contain\nthe states of the various workspaces that will subsequently be created for\nthis configuration.\n\n### Environment Account Setup", "numLines": 100, "startLine": 300, "totalLines": 634}}

arguments.content:
# Terraform v1.9.0 to v1.10.0 Migration Guide

## Overview

Terraform v1.10.0 is a minor release in the stable v1.0 series and honors the [Terraform v1.0 Compatibility Promises](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/v1-compatibility-promises). However, this release includes breaking changes in the S3 backend configuration and `moved` block syntax that require manual action before or during upgrade, as well as important behavioral changes to sensitive value propagation and a significant new feature (ephemeral resources and values) that changes how secret data should be handled going forward.

**Key themes:**
- **Breaking:** S3 backend removes deprecated root-level IAM role assumption attributes
- **Breaking:** `moved` blocks now require `resource.` prefix for types that conflict with reserved keywords
- **Behavioral:** Sensitive marks now consistently propagate through conditional and `for` expressions
- **New:** Ephemeral resources, input variables, and outputs for secret handling without state persistence

---

## Breaking Changes

### 1. S3 Backend: Removal of Root-Level IAM Role Assumption Attributes

#### What Changed and Why

Several root-level attributes for IAM role assumption in the S3 backend that were previously deprecated have been **permanently removed** in v1.10.0. These attributes have had block-based replacements since earlier Terraform versions, and the migration was deferred until this release.

The removed attributes and their replacements are:

| Removed Attribute | Replacement (inside `assume_role` block) |
|---|---|
| `role_arn` | `assume_role.role_arn` |
| `session_name` | `assume_role.session_name` |
| `external_id` | `assume_role.external_id` |
| `assume_role_duration_seconds` | `assume_role.duration` |
| `assume_role_policy` | `assume_role.policy` |
| `assume_role_policy_arns` | `assume_role.policy_arns` |
| `assume_role_tags` | `assume_role.tags` |
| `assume_role_transitive_tag_keys` | `assume_role.transitive_tag_keys` |

Note that `assume_role_duration_seconds` maps to `duration` (not `duration_seconds`) in the block syntax, and accepts a duration string (e.g. `"1h30m"`) rather than an integer number of seconds.

#### Before/After Code Examples

**Before (v1.9.0 — using deprecated root-level attributes):**

```hcl
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket       = "my-terraform-state"
    key          = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region       = "us-east-1"

    # These root-level attributes are REMOVED in v1.10.0
    role_arn                   = "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/TerraformRole"
    session_name               = "terraform-session"
    external_id                = "my-external-id"
    assume_role_duration_seconds = 3600
    assume_role_policy         = "{\"Version\":\"2012-10-17\",\"Statement\":[]}"
    assume_role_policy_arns    = ["arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/ReadOnlyAccess"]
    assume_role_tags           = { Environment = "prod" }
    assume_role_transitive_tag_keys = ["Environment"]
  }
}
```

**After (v1.10.0 — using `assume_role` block):**

```hcl
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket = "my-terraform-state"
    key    = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"

    assume_role = {
      role_arn               = "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/TerraformRole"
      session_name           = "terraform-session"
      external_id            = "my-external-id"
      duration               = "1h"   # Note: string format, not seconds integer
      policy                 = "{\"Version\":\"2012-10-17\",\"Statement\":[]}"
      policy_arns            = ["arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/ReadOnlyAccess"]
      tags                   = { Environment = "prod" }
      transitive_tag_keys    = ["Environment"]
    }
  }
}
```

#### Step-by-Step Migration Instructions

1. **Identify affected configurations:** Search all your Terraform configuration files for any `backend "s3"` blocks that contain root-level `role_arn`, `session_name`, `external_id`, `assume_role_duration_seconds`, `assume_role_policy`, `assume_role_policy_arns`, `assume_role_tags`, or `assume_role_transitive_tag_keys` attributes.

   ```bash
   grep -rn "role_arn\|session_name\|external_id\|assume_role_duration_seconds\|assume_role_policy\|assume_role_tags\|assume_role_transitive_tag_keys" . --include="*.tf" --include="*.tfbackend"
   ```

2. **Update each backend configuration:** Move all the matching root-level attributes inside an `assume_role = { ... }` block. Convert `assume_role_duration_seconds` (integer) to `duration` (duration string, e.g. `3600` seconds becomes `"1h"`).

3. **Handle partial configurations:** If your backend uses `-backend-config` flags or partial configuration files (`.tfbackend`), update those files as well. The block syntax must be used in all forms.

4. **Re-initialize:** After updating the configuration, run `terraform init` to validate the new backend configuration:

   ```bash
   terraform init
   ```

5. **Verify:** Confirm that `terraform plan` runs without errors.

#### Common Pitfalls

- **Duration format change:** `assume_role_duration_seconds` accepted an integer (e.g. `3600`), but `duration` in the `assume_role` block requires a duration string (e.g. `"1h"`, `"90m"`, `"1h30m"`). Forgetting this conversion will cause a validation error.
- **Partial configuration files:** Backend configuration supplied via `-backend-config` files must also be updated to use the block syntax.
- **Multiple `assume_role` blocks:** The `assume_role` argument supports multiple values in v1.10.0 — roles are assumed in order. If you were chaining role assumptions, you can now express this as a list.

---

### 2. `moved` Blocks: Required `resource.` Prefix for Reserved Type Names

#### What Changed and Why

In v1.10.0, `moved` blocks now correctly parse resource addresses using the same rules as the rest of the Terraform language. This means reserved keywords and top-level block types (such as `local`, `each`, `self`, `count`, `path`, `module`, `var`, `data`, `resource`, `provider`, `terraform`, `check`, and similar) are now recognized as keywords rather than resource type names when they appear as the first segment of an address in `from` or `to` fields.

If you have a resource with a **type name** that matches one of these reserved words (e.g., `resource "local" "config" {}`), you must now use the `resource.` prefix in any `moved` block that references it.

#### Before/After Code Examples

**Scenario:** A resource with type `local` (an unusual but valid type name from a custom provider).

**Before (v1.9.0 — worked, now errors in v1.10.0):**

```hcl
# Resource definition
resource "local" "config" {
  # ...
}

# This moved block previously parsed "local.config" as resource type "local", name "config"
moved {
  from = local.config
  to   = local.settings
}
```

**After (v1.10.0 — must use `resource.` prefix):**

```hcl
# Resource definition
resource "local" "config" {
  # ...
}

# Must now explicitly qualify with "resource." when the type matches a keyword
moved {
  from = resource.local.config
  to   = resource.local.settings
}
```

#### Step-by-Step Migration Instructions

1. **Identify affected `moved` blocks:** Search for `moved` blocks in your configurations and check whether the resource type name in the `from` or `to` fields matches any reserved keyword.

   Reserved keywords include (but are not limited to): `local`, `locals`, `each`, `self`, `count`, `path`, `module`, `var`, `data`, `resource`, `provider`, `terraform`, `check`, `import`, `removed`, `moved`.

   ```bash
   grep -rn "moved {" . --include="*.tf" -A 5
   ```

2. **Check for conflicts:** For each `moved` block, examine the `from` and `to` addresses. If the first segment (the type name) matches a reserved word, add the `resource.` prefix.

3. **Test with `terraform validate`:** Run `terraform validate` after updating to confirm there are no remaining errors:

   ```bash
   terraform validate
   ```

4. **Run a plan to confirm:** Execute `terraform plan` to verify that Terraform correctly identifies the moved resources and proposes no unexpected changes:

   ```bash
   terraform plan
   ```

#### Common Pitfalls

- **Custom providers with generic type names:** Some older or community providers use type names like `local`, `data`, or `module` which can conflict. These configurations need the `resource.` qualifier.
- **Module-level `moved` blocks:** This applies to `moved` blocks in both root modules and child modules. Check all modules in your configuration.
- **The `resource.` prefix is always valid:** Even in cases where the prefix was not previously required, adding `resource.` to a resource address in a `moved` block is valid in all Terraform v1.x versions. If you are unsure whether a type name conflicts, adding the prefix is safe.

---

## Behavioral Changes

### 3. Sensitive Value Handling: Consistent Mark Propagation in Expressions

#### What Changed and Why

Prior to v1.10.0, there was a bug where sensitive marks (the internal mechanism that causes Terraform to redact values shown as `(sensitive value)`) were **incorrectly dropped** in certain conditional expressions (`condition ? true_val : false_val`) and `for` expressions when one of the branches or elements involved an unknown value. This could cause two problems:

1. **Mark loss:** A value that should have been marked sensitive was silently treated as non-sensitive, potentially exposing secret data in plan output.
2. **Erroneous plan changes:** Sensitive marks were sometimes incorrectly transferred to unrelated nested resource values, causing Terraform to plan spurious changes.

In v1.10.0, both issues are fixed. Sensitive marks now propagate correctly through all expression types. The result is:

- Expressions that involve a sensitive value will consistently produce a sensitive result.
- You may see **previously hidden** plan changes become visible now that incorrect mark propagation no longer masks them.
- Some expressions that previously appeared non-sensitive may now correctly appear as `(sensitive value)` in plan output.

#### When Sensitive Marks Are Now Preserved

In v1.10.0, the sensitive mark is preserved in all of the following cases (some of which previously dropped the mark):

```hcl
# If var.secret is sensitive, the result of a conditional expression
# is now consistently marked sensitive, even when one branch is unknown.
local.value = var.some_condition ? var.secret : "default"

# In for expressions, sensitivity is properly propagated
local.filtered = [for item in var.sensitive_list : item if item != ""]

# Conditional with unknown condition is now correctly sensitive
local.result = var.unknown_flag ? var.secret : var.other_secret
```

#### How to Use `nonsensitive()` to Override When Needed

If the consistent propagation causes `(sensitive value)` to appear where you know the resulting value is safe to expose, use the `nonsensitive()` function to explicitly unwrap the sensitivity mark:

```hcl
# Before v1.10.0: this might have accidentally exposed the value as non-sensitive
# After v1.10.0: the result is correctly marked sensitive
local.public_info = var.is_public ? var.api_endpoint : var.secret_endpoint

# If you are certain the output is safe to display, use nonsensitive()
output "safe_endpoint" {
  value = nonsensitive(var.is_public ? var.api_endpoint : var.secret_endpoint)
}
```

**Only use `nonsensitive()` when you are certain the value does not contain sensitive data.** Misuse can expose secret values in plan output, state files, and logs.

#### Migration Steps

1. **Run `terraform plan` after upgrading** and review any new `(sensitive value)` annotations or any plan changes that were previously hidden. These may indicate legitimate changes that were previously masked.

2. **Review output blocks** that reference conditional expressions involving sensitive variables. If the output is now unexpectedly marked sensitive, you have two options:
   - Declare the output as `sensitive = true` to explicitly acknowledge it.
   - Restructure the expression to avoid mixing sensitive and non-sensitive paths, or use `nonsensitive()` if appropriate.

3. **Review resource configurations** that produce unexpected plan changes. If plan changes disappear after upgrade, they may have been artifacts of the previous incorrect mark propagation behavior. Conversely, new plan changes may reflect previously masked real differences.

#### Common Pitfalls

- **`nonsensitive()` does not remove sensitivity from state:** The `nonsensitive()` function only affects plan/apply output display. Sensitive values are still stored in state unless you use ephemeral values (see below).
- **Unexpected plan diffs:** If you see new plan changes after upgrading that relate to sensitive resource attributes, investigate whether the change is real or was previously masked. Consider using `terraform show` or `terraform state show` to examine the current state.

---

## New Features with Migration Impact

### 4. Ephemeral Resources and Values

Terraform v1.10.0 introduces **ephemeral resources, ephemeral input variables, and ephemeral outputs** as a new mechanism for handling secrets that should never be persisted to state or plan files. This is a public beta feature and the workflows may evolve in future releases.

> **Note:** Ephemeral resources, variables, and outputs are in public beta in v1.10.0. Provider support for ephemeral resources requires providers that implement the new ephemeral resource type.

#### What Is Ephemeral?

Ephemeral values exist only in memory during a single Terraform operation (plan or apply). Unlike `sensitive` values, which are stored in state (just redacted in output), **ephemeral values are never written to state files, plan files, or logs**.

Use ephemeral values for:
- Short-lived authentication tokens (e.g. Vault leases, OIDC tokens)
- Database passwords that should not persist in state
- API keys that rotate frequently
- Any credential that should exist only for the duration of a Terraform run

#### Ephemeral Input Variables

Mark an input variable as ephemeral to prevent it from being stored in state:

```hcl
variable "db_password" {
  type      = string
  ephemeral = true
  sensitive = true  # Also mark sensitive to suppress CLI output
}
```

Ephemeral variables can only be referenced in:
- Other ephemeral variables
- Local values (which become implicitly ephemeral)
- Ephemeral resources
- Ephemeral outputs
- Provider configuration blocks
- Provisioner and connection blocks

**If you reference an ephemeral variable in a non-ephemeral context, Terraform will return an error.**

#### Ephemeral Outputs (Child Modules Only)

Mark an output in a **child module** as ephemeral to pass secrets between modules without persisting them:

```hcl
# modules/db/main.tf
output "connection_string" {
  value     = "postgres://${var.username}:${var.db_password}@${var.host}:5432/${var.database}"
  ephemeral = true
  sensitive = true
}
```

> **Important:** Ephemeral outputs are only supported in child modules. You cannot mark a root module output as `ephemeral`.

#### Ephemeral Resources (Provider Support Required)

Providers that support ephemeral resources expose them via a new `ephemeral` block type. Ephemeral resources are opened, optionally renewed, and closed during each Terraform operation — their data is never stored in state:

```hcl
# Retrieve a secret from AWS Secrets Manager without storing it in state
ephemeral "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "db_credentials" {
  secret_id = data.aws_db_instance.example.master_user_secret[0].secret_arn
}

locals {
  credentials = jsondecode(ephemeral.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.db_credentials.secret_string)
}

provider "postgresql" {
  host     = data.aws_db_instance.example.address
  port     = data.aws_db_instance.example.port
  username = local.credentials["username"]
  password = local.credentials["password"]
}
```

#### The `ephemeralasnull` Function

A new built-in function `ephemeralasnull()` converts ephemeral values to `null`, allowing you to output or use the non-ephemeral parts of a mixed object:

```hcl
variable "session_token" {
  type      = string
  ephemeral = true
}

variable "environment" {
  type    = string
  default = "production"
}

locals {
  config = {
    environment   = var.environment
    session_token = var.session_token  # ephemeral
  }
}

# Output only the non-ephemeral parts; session_token becomes null
output "safe_config" {
  value = ephemeralasnull(local.config)
}
```

#### Migration Impact: Sensitive vs. Ephemeral

Before v1.10.0, the only way to protect secrets was `sensitive = true`, which hides values in output but **still stores them in state**. Starting with v1.10.0, you have a stronger guarantee with ephemeral values:

| Property | `sensitive = true` | `ephemeral = true` |
|---|---|---|
| Hidden in plan/apply output | Yes | Yes (never shown) |
| Stored in state file | **Yes** | **No** |
| Stored in plan file | **Yes** | **No** |
| Available in next run from state | Yes | No (must be re-provided) |
| Can be used in regular resources | Yes | No (ephemeral contexts only) |

**When to use which:**
- Use `sensitive` for values that must persist in state (e.g., resource IDs that happen to be sensitive)
- Use `ephemeral` for credentials, tokens, and passwords that should never touch state

#### IAM and Bucket Policy Updates for S3 Locking

When enabling S3 native state locking (`use_lockfile = true`), the lock file is placed alongside the state file with a `.tflock` extension. Update your S3 bucket policies and IAM policies to include permissions for the lock file path:

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": ["s3:GetObject", "s3:PutObject", "s3:DeleteObject"],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/path/to/my/key",
        "arn:aws:s3:::mybucket/path/to/my/key.tflock"
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

#### Opting Into S3 Native State Locking

S3 native locking is opt-in and experimental in v1.10.0. To enable it:

```hcl
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket   = "my-terraform-state"
    key      = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region   = "us-east-1"

    # Experimental S3 native locking (opt-in)
    use_lockfile = true

    # DynamoDB locking can run alongside S3 locking during transition
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-state-lock"
  }
}
```

When both `use_lockfile` and `dynamodb_table` are configured, **both locks must be acquired** before an operation proceeds. This allows a gradual migration from DynamoDB-only locking to S3-only locking.

> **Note:** In a future Terraform minor version, DynamoDB-based locking will be deprecated and S3 native locking will become the standard. Plan your migration path accordingly.

---

## Testing Your Migration

Follow these steps to validate a successful upgrade from v1.9.0 to v1.10.0:

### 1. Update Your Terraform Version

```bash
# Verify the installed version
terraform version
# Should show: Terraform v1.10.x
```

If using a version constraint in your configuration, update it:

```hcl
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.10.0"
}
```

### 2. Address Breaking Changes First

Before running any Terraform commands:

1. Update all S3 backend configurations to remove deprecated root-level IAM attributes (see [S3 Backend Changes](#1-s3-backend-removal-of-root-level-iam-role-assumption-attributes))
2. Update any `moved` blocks that reference resource types with reserved keyword names (see [`moved` Block Changes](#2-moved-blocks-required-resource-prefix-for-reserved-type-names))

### 3. Re-Initialize

```bash
terraform init -upgrade
```

This re-downloads providers and validates the backend configuration.

### 4. Validate Configuration

```bash
terraform validate
```

Address any errors or warnings before proceeding. Warnings about deprecated attributes (like `dynamodb_table` or `shared_credentials_file`) can be resolved at your discretion but are not blocking.

### 5. Run a Plan and Review

```bash
terraform plan -out=upgrade-plan.tfplan
```

Review the plan carefully for:

- Any **new `(sensitive value)` annotations** — these are now correctly marked; verify they are expected
- Any **new resource changes** that were not expected — these may have been masked by the previous mark propagation bug
- Ensure no resource **replacements** are proposed unexpectedly

### 6. Apply the Plan

If the plan looks correct:

```bash
terraform apply upgrade-plan.tfplan
```

### 7. Verify State

```bash
terraform show
```

Confirm that the state reflects the expected infrastructure state.

### 8. Test Sensitive Value Behavior (if applicable)

If your configuration uses sensitive variables with conditional expressions, verify the behavior:

```bash
terraform plan -var="secret_value=test-value"
```

Confirm that sensitive values appear as `(sensitive value)` in the output where expected.

---

## Rollback Guidance

If you encounter issues after upgrading to v1.10.0 and need to revert:

### Prerequisites Before Upgrading

Before upgrading, take these precautions:

1. **Back up your state files:** Copy your state files to a safe location before running `terraform apply` with v1.10.0.

   ```bash
   # For local state
   cp terraform.tfstate terraform.tfstate.v1.9-backup

   # For S3 backend — enable versioning if not already enabled
   aws s3api put-bucket-versioning \
     --bucket my-terraform-state \
     --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled
   ```

2. **Record your current state version:** Note the state serial number with `terraform show -json | jq '.values.root_module'` before upgrading.

### Rolling Back the Terraform Binary

1. Reinstall Terraform v1.9.x:

   ```bash
   # Using tfenv
   tfenv install 1.9.8
   tfenv use 1.9.8

   # Or download directly from releases.hashicorp.com
   ```

2. Revert your configuration changes (restore deprecated attribute usage in S3 backend, remove `resource.` prefixes from `moved` blocks if needed).

3. Run `terraform init` to re-initialize with the older version.

### Rolling Back S3 Native Locking

If you enabled `use_lockfile = true` and need to roll back:

1. Ensure no Terraform operations are in progress (check for `.tflock` files in your S3 bucket).
2. Remove `use_lockfile = true` from your backend configuration.
3. Run `terraform init` to re-initialize.
4. If any `.tflock` files remain in S3 from a failed operation, remove them manually before running Terraform again:

   ```bash
   aws s3 rm s3://my-bucket/path/to/state.tflock
   ```

### Rolling Back State If Needed

If a `terraform apply` with v1.10.0 produced unexpected changes:

1. Use `terraform state` commands to inspect and correct state:

   ```bash
   terraform state list
   terraform state show <resource_address>
   ```

2. For S3 backends with versioning enabled, restore a previous state version:

   ```bash
   aws s3api list-object-versions \
     --bucket my-terraform-state \
     --prefix path/to/my/key

   aws s3api get-object \
     --bucket my-terraform-state \
     --key path/to/my/key \
     --version-id <version-id> \
     terraform.tfstate.restored
   ```

3. Push the restored state:

   ```bash
   terraform state push terraform.tfstate.restored
   ```

### Important Rollback Caveats

- If you used ephemeral resources or variables during the upgraded run, rolling back to v1.9.0 means those features will no longer be available. You must refactor configurations back to use `sensitive` variables.
- The `assume_role` block syntax for the S3 backend is supported in v1.9.x. If you have already migrated to the block syntax, you do not need to revert to the deprecated root-level attributes.
- S3 native locking (`.tflock` files) is only understood by Terraform v1.10.0+. If rolling back to v1.9.x, ensure any lock files are cleaned up first.

---

## Summary of Changes at a Glance

| Change | Type | Action Required |
|--------|------|-----------------|
| S3 backend root-level IAM attributes removed | **Breaking** | Must migrate to `assume_role` block |
| `moved` blocks require `resource.` prefix for reserved type names | **Breaking** | Update `moved` blocks if affected |
| Sensitive marks propagate correctly in conditional/for expressions | Behavioral (bug fix) | Review plan output for new sensitive annotations or unexpected changes |
| Ephemeral resources, variables, outputs (beta) | New Feature | Optional; adopt for improved secret management |
| S3 native state locking via `use_lockfile` (experimental) | New Feature | Optional; consider for migration away from DynamoDB locking |
| `ephemeralasnull()` function | New Feature | Optional; use with ephemeral values |

For further help, see the [official Terraform upgrade guide](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/upgrade-guides) or start a discussion in the [Terraform community forum](https://discuss.hashicorp.com/c/terraform-core).
